Reference: Hansen et al. Sports Med. 2018.[555] Q. How does salt increase blood pressure? Sodium is a known regulator of blood pressure. Sodium concentrations are sensed by macula densa cells in the kidneys.[556] When the blood sodium concentration increases, these cells activate the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system, as shown below. 340

In short, increased salt intake causes your body to hold onto more water, which increases blood pressure. This extra pressure places a strain on your cardiovascular system which can eventually lead to worse cardiovascular disease outcomes over time. Sodium’s role in blood pressure Q. How does baseline blood pressure modify the effects of salt reduction? Sodium restriction may reduce both systolic and diastolic blood pressure, primarily among people starting out with higher blood pressure. Although there can be small benefits among people with lower blood pressure, these effects are either not clinically relevant (and possibly non-existent). The effects of a reduction in salt intake of 2,300 milligrams (2.3 g) stratified by blood pressure are summarized below. Changes in blood pressure to salt reduction, by baseline status 341

Q. Can red meat cause heart disease? There is some evidence that eating a lot of red meat or processed meat might increase the risk of cardiovascular diseases, but that evidence tends to be of lower certainty.[557][558][559] Yet, given the difficult nature of conducting long-term nutrition trials, we aren’t likely to get any truly high-quality evidence anytime soon. So, what are we to make of the studies we have, low-quality though they may be? Remember that red and processed meat consumption is just a small part of the overall lifestyle factors that will influence your heart health. If you moderate your red meat intake, exercise regularly, eat your fruits and veggies, consume adequate fiber, don’t smoke, and drink alcohol only in moderation, red meat’s effect on your heart health isn’t something to worry too much about. Still, if you wish to be cautious, you can limit your intake to three servings per week (1 serving of meat = 3 oz = 85 grams). Prioritize limiting the kind of meat that has been cured, smoked, or highly processed. Q. Why take NAC to make glutathione? Why not take glutathione directly? Oral glutathione gets digested into its constituent amino acids: cysteine, glycin

t has been cured, smoked, or highly processed. Q. Why take NAC to make glutathione? Why not take glutathione directly? Oral glutathione gets digested into its constituent amino acids: cysteine, glycine, and glutamic acid. Of those three, cysteine is the rate-limiting factor in endogenous glutathione production. Oral N- Acetylcysteine (NAC) is simply a more efficient (and cheaper) way of providing your body with cysteine. Multiple studies have reported greater increases in circulating glutathione from oral NAC than from (an equal dose of) oral glutathione. 342

Dieting FAQ Each year we receive hundreds of emails with questions on dieting, from inquiries on specific types of diets to requests for tips on diet adherence. We can’t possibly answer all the questions every Fitness Guide reader may have, but we came up with answers to ten questions we often encounter. Fine details aside, always keep in mind that diet sustainability (how well you can stick to a given healthy diet) is the most important component of a weight-loss program. Table of Contents • Does meal timing influence weight loss? • Does meal frequency influence weight loss? • Does food processing influence weight loss? • Low-carb or low-fat, which is better for weight loss? • Does protein promote weight loss? • What is the best diet for weight loss? • What is the best diet for my health? • What are the potential benefits (and risks) of fasting? • Is it easier to regain weight if you lost it quickly? • How much should you walk while on a diet? Q. Does meal timing influence weight loss? Our circadian rhythm operates under alternating periods of light and dark. Light is connected with activity and feeding, whereas darkness signals resting and fasting. Unsurprisingly, eating late at

? Our circadian rhythm operates under alternating periods of light and dark. Light is connected with activity and feeding, whereas darkness signals resting and fasting. Unsurprisingly, eating late at night has been associated with weight gain: night-shift workers are at an increased risk of obesity, especially abdominal obesity.[578] This phenomenon is the result of many factors affected by circadian rhythms, such as poor sleep,[579] metabolic disturbances,[580] and unhealthy dietary choices.[581] During a 20-week intervention, early eaters lost weight faster than late eaters, despite similar appetites, diet types, and energy intakes and expenditures.[582] In another controlled trial, obese 343

women who ate a greater portion of their calories at breakfast lost 2.5 times as much weight as those who ate a greater portion of the same amount of calories at dinner.[583] Those two studies, however, used a mix of subjective or pseudo-subjective measures of energy expenditure and food intake. To this day, the bulk of the evidence indicates that how much and what matter much more than when: you need to eat less than you burn to lose weight, and you need to eat enough protein so that the weight you lose will be fat rather than muscle. The timing of your intake of protein and carbs can matter around exercise. As for regular meals, if eating late increases neither your daily caloric intake nor your daily intake of unhealthy food, it won’t likely change how much weight you gain or lose. So if you’d rather skip breakfast and allocate those calories to other meals, you can. Q. Does meal frequency influence weight loss? Everything else being equal, a bigger meal will produce a greater thermogenic response, but given the same daily caloric load and macronutrient content, a few big meals will have the same cumulative thermogenic response as will many small meals — as was demonstrated with

ogenic response, but given the same daily caloric load and macronutrient content, a few big meals will have the same cumulative thermogenic response as will many small meals — as was demonstrated with one vs. two meals,[584] two vs. three meals,[585] and two vs. seven meals.[586] Digging Deeper: Diet-induced thermogenesis Some of the calories in the food you ingest will be used to digest, absorb, and metabolize the rest of the food, and some will be burned off as heat. This process is known under various names, notably diet-induced thermogenesis (DIT), thermic effect of food (TEF), and specific dynamic action (SDA).[587] DIT represents about 10% of the caloric intake of healthy adults eating a standard mixed diet,[588] but your actual number will depend on several factors, which include your lean body mass and the size and composition of your meal. The energy required to digest each macronutrient (its DIT) can be expressed as a percentage of the energy provided by this macronutrient:[589] • Fat provides 9 Calories per gram, and its DIT is 0–3%. 344
89] • Fat provides 9 Calories per gram, and its DIT is 0–3%. 344

• Carbohydrate provides 4 Calories per gram, and its DIT is 5–10%. • Protein provides 4 Calories per gram, and its DIT is 20–30%. So you won’t burn more if you eat a few big meals than if you eat numerous small meals. Yet meal frequency does matter, but for personal reasons, some psychological, some practical. • Psychological: Does eating more often make you more or less likely to overeat? Does it make you more or less likely to favor unhealthy foods? • Practical: Can you bring healthy snacks to work, if you want to eat more often? Or would you have to rely on your floor’s snack machine? Can you at least bring your own lunch? Or are you expected to share a hearty meal with your colleagues at a local restaurant? People’s preferences and circumstances are too varied for specific advice, so you’ll have to try different approaches and pick the one that works best. Q. Does food processing influence weight loss? Processed foods promote weight gain and hinder weight loss,[590][591][592][593] for at least three reasons: • For the same macronutrient content, less processing means greater diet-induced thermogenesis (DIT). You need to burn more calories to digest multigrain bread with cheddar

for at least three reasons: • For the same macronutrient content, less processing means greater diet-induced thermogenesis (DIT). You need to burn more calories to digest multigrain bread with cheddar cheese, for instance, than to digest white bread with processed cheese.[594] • The easier a food is to eat and digest, the less satiating it is. For the same amount of calories, a solid (apple) will reduce appetite more than a semisolid (applesauce), and a semi-solid more than a liquid (apple juice).[595] This is true even if you add the lost fiber back into the juice.[596] • Processed foods tend to be calorie-dense. Per 100 grams, apples provide 52 Calories, whereas apple pies provide 237 Calories. Soups tend to have low caloric density (100 grams of chicken soup provides only 61 Calories), which is probably why they’re highly satiating despite being liquids.[597] 345

Digging Deeper: The Volumetrics diet The Volumetrics diet is based on the satiating properties of foods with low caloric density — foods with high volume for their caloric content, so foods rich in fiber and water.[598][599][600] Simply eating a salad at the start of a meal can reduce your caloric intake for the whole meal.[601] In studies, telling overweight or obese women to increase their intake of low-caloric-density foods (e.g., fruit, vegetables, and soups) led to less hunger, lower caloric intake, and greater weight loss than telling them to reduce their caloric intake or their intake of fat-rich foods. [602][603] Q. Low-carb or low-fat, which is better for weight loss? For decades, fat was the demon: eating fat was what made you fat. Then, in a dramatic twist, fat was revealed to be a scapegoat for the real saboteur: carbs. If you eat more carbs, you produce more insulin, and insulin is what makes you fat, according to the Carbohydrate-Insulin Model of Obesity (CIMO) — which has recently fallen into disfavor,[604][605] though it still has its proponents. [606] So, where do we stand now? Still in the midst of a seemingly unending tennis rally. If one study reports greater we

s recently fallen into disfavor,[604][605] though it still has its proponents. [606] So, where do we stand now? Still in the midst of a seemingly unending tennis rally. If one study reports greater weight loss from low-carb,[607] the next reports greater weight loss from low-fat.[608] The best trial to date lasted 12 months: called DIETFITS, it found that a healthy low-carb diet and a healthy low-fat diet led to similar weight changes. Further, it noted that “neither genotype pattern nor baseline insulin secretion was associated with the dietary effects on weight loss”.[406] It is important to note that, like other studies,[609][610] DIETFITS found the weight-loss difference between the low-carb group and the low-fat group to be very small compared to the weight-loss variations within a same group. In other words, interindividual differences seem to matter more than the diet being low-fat or low-carb. Weight change after 12 months for each participant 346

Adapted from Gardner et al. JAMA. 2018.[406] In fact, when compiling the data, we realize that low-fat or low-carb may not even matter at all in the long run. Whereas a 2015 meta-analysis of diet trials reported modestly greater weight loss from low-carb diets,[611] a meta-analysis of diet trials published the year before had found that, after a year, low-carb and low-fat diets equalized.[612] Low-carb diets vs. Low-fat diets Reference: Johnston et al. JAMA. 2014.[612] 347

Now, these studies all give us great information on the real-world effects of low-fat and low-carb diets, but they have their limitations: food intake was not controlled and diets were self-reported. Also, in some of them, there were differences in energy and protein intake between groups, making it impossible to isolate the fat and carb contributions to the findings. Therefore, it is notable that a meta-analysis of 32 studies whose participants were fed by the researchers (thus ensuring that, within each study, each diet had different amounts of fat and carbs but the same amount of protein and calories) reported that low-fat diets resulted in greater energy expenditure (by an average of 26 Calories per day) and greater fat loss (by an average of 16 grams per day).[613] The diets varied widely in carbs (1–83% of calories) and fat (4–84% of calories — so yes, ketogenic diets were included). An extra 16 grams of fat loss per day amounts to about a pound of fat each month, so 12 pounds a year. But don’t declare victory for low-fat yet — these were highly controlled feeding studies, and real life isn’t highly controlled. In the real world, some people will lose more weight on a low-car

year. But don’t declare victory for low-fat yet — these were highly controlled feeding studies, and real life isn’t highly controlled. In the real world, some people will lose more weight on a low-carb diet, due to factors such as limiting junk food choices and encouraging higher protein intake. In sum, low-fat and low-carb diets produce similar weight-loss results, at least in long-term clinical trials. Both types of diet work mostly because the trials’ participants reduce their intake of calories, not just of carbs or fat, and because eating less carbohydrate or fat usually results in eating more protein. Q. Does protein promote weight loss? Yes, for two reasons: higher diet-induced thermogenesis (DIT) and greater satiety. You need more calories to digest protein than to digest carbs or fat. Granted, the effect of shifting your caloric intake to protein seems puny: for every 1% increase in calories from protein (and concomitant decrease in calories from carbs and fat), you can expect a 0.22% increase in your meal’s total DIT.[614] However, the studies used to calculate this increase averaged a protein intake of only 16% of daily calories, with only 3 of the 19 studies using a pro

a 0.22% increase in your meal’s total DIT.[614] However, the studies used to calculate this increase averaged a protein intake of only 16% of daily calories, with only 3 of the 19 studies using a protein intake greater than 20%. Larger amounts of protein could have a different effect. In addition, eating more protein can decrease your appetite, for protein is especially satiating[615] [616] when it makes for 25–81% of a meal’s calories.[617] One possible reason, especially for women, is protein’s higher DIT.[618][619][620] Another reason is that amino acids, especially leucine, directly tell your brain that you’re being fed, both by suppressing AMPK (a “starvation-sensing” enzyme) in your hypothalamus (your brain’s master appetite regulator) and by stimulating the mTOR signaling pathway (which is involved in protein synthesis, cell growth, and reproduction).[621] [622][623] 348

These mechanistic benefits mirror benefits seen in clinical trials.[624][625][626][627] For example, a study had its 19 participants each follow three diets successively.[628] High-protein diet vs. high-fat diet CALORIES DURATION DIET Weight maintenance 2 weeks High in fat (15% protein, 35% fat, 50% carbs) Weight maintenance 2 weeks High in protein (30% protein, 20% fat, 50% carbs) Ad libitum 12 weeks High in protein (30% protein, 20% fat, 50% carbs) Reference: Weigle et al. Am J Clin Nutr. 2005.[628] Of the two weight-maintenance diets, the high-protein diet led to more satiety. Moreover, during the 12 weeks during which the study participants could eat as much as they wanted (within the constraint of a high-protein diet), their energy intake decreased spontaneously by a mean 441 Calories — in other words, they naturally ate less than they burned. This study and others report greater weight loss from protein-rich diets,[629][630][631] but not all of them do. What all such studies report, however, is that protein promotes fat loss over muscle loss. [632][633][634][635][636][637][638][639] Q. What is the best diet for weight loss? There are three main types of diet: • Intermittent f

rt, however, is that protein promotes fat loss over muscle loss. [632][633][634][635][636][637][638][639] Q. What is the best diet for weight loss? There are three main types of diet: • Intermittent fasting (IF) diets limit when you can eat. • Chronic caloric restriction (CCR) diets limit how much you can eat. • Food restriction (FR) diets limit what you can eat. IF diets require that you refrain from eating (for sixteen hours straight each day, for instance, or for two days per week). CCR diets require that you eat fewer meals or smaller meals. And FR diets, 349

such as the Volumetrics diet, require that you favor some foods over others. IF and FR diets can be combined: you can refrain from eating certain foods for most of the day or week. This can be a permanent “deal” or just a way to ease yourself into a healthy diet — you could refrain from eating cookies and candies for one day each week, then two, and so on. In a typical cyclical ketogenic diet, you refrain from eating carbs during the workweek then refill your glycogen stores over the weekend. IF and CCR diets can also be combined: a form of Alternate-Day Fasting has you eat normally over 24 hours then very little (about 500 Calories) over the next 24 hours. CCR and FR diets are often combined — for instance, some diets require that you reduce your caloric intake (CCR diet) by reducing your fat intake (FR diet). Low-fat diets and low-carb diets generate similar weight loss, according to the bulk of the evidence, as do 11 brand-name diets, according to a 2014 meta-analysis (which included only brand-name diets, so not, for instance, the Mediterranean diet). Furthermore, this meta-analysis found no association between a diet’s effect on weight loss and the dieter’s genotype pattern or

d only brand-name diets, so not, for instance, the Mediterranean diet). Furthermore, this meta-analysis found no association between a diet’s effect on weight loss and the dieter’s genotype pattern or baseline insulin. The researchers concluded that “because different diets are variably tolerated by individuals, the ideal diet is one that is best adhered to by individuals so they can stay on the diet as long as possible”.[612] In short, the best diet is the healthy diet you can stick to. Q. What is the best diet for my health? In overweight people, fat loss will improve most health markers; whatever specific advantages low- carb might have over low-fat, and vice versa, are secondary. Moreover, macronutrients are not uniform categories: your body won’t react to 30 grams of sugar as it will to 30 grams of slow- digesting carbs; it won’t even react to 30 grams of sugar from a soda as it will from 30 grams of sugar from a whole apple. That being said, if you’re diabetic or at risk of diabetes, you may consider trying a high-protein,[640] low-carb diet[641][642][643][644][645][646][647][648] (one study limited daily carbs to 20% of calories,[649] others to between 20 grams[650][651] and

tes, you may consider trying a high-protein,[640] low-carb diet[641][642][643][644][645][646][647][648] (one study limited daily carbs to 20% of calories,[649] others to between 20 grams[650][651] and 90 grams,[652] depending on the study) or at least a low-GI diet.[653][654][655][656][657][658][659][660] If you’re overweight but not diabetic, though, switching from a high-GI diet to a low-GI diet won’t likely improve your insulin sensitivity, lipid levels, or systolic blood pressure.[661] But for heart health, low-fat diets are better, right? Well … maybe. But for heart health, low-fat diets are better, right? Well … maybe. Those on a low-carb or ketogenic diet can experience shifts in their blood lipid and cholesterol profiles. Triglycerides commonly drop, while high-density 350

lipoproteins (HDL-C) and low-density lipoproteins (LDL-C) usually increase.[662][663][664] Both HDL and LDL particles have been seen to increase and remain elevated on a long-term keto diet (2 years).[662] LDL particles are one part of the heart disease picture. Several events need to occur for heart disease to develop. This helps explain why heart disease has numerous risk factors, such as diabetes, obesity, smoking, lack of exercise, and infection.[507] While the full implications of having elevated LDL while on a low-carb diet are not fully known, it would be prudent to keep an eye on measurements. Of course, we should remember that macronutrients aren’t uniform categories: the kind of fat you consume matters. Trans fat is better avoided, but fish oil may benefit heart health,[665][666] and saturated fat isn’t the dietary devil it was made out to be.[667][498][668][669] Let’s mention a few specialized diets, designed to address specific issues: • The ketogenic diet (a very-low-carb, high-fat, moderate-protein diet) is well established as therapy for intractable epilepsy, and it may help against other neurological diseases.[670] • The Dietary Approach to Stop Hypertension (DASH)

high-fat, moderate-protein diet) is well established as therapy for intractable epilepsy, and it may help against other neurological diseases.[670] • The Dietary Approach to Stop Hypertension (DASH) aims to lower high blood pressure.[671] • The autoimmune protocol (AIP) can improve symptoms and reduce endoscopic inflammation in patients with inflammatory bowel disease.[672] • The low-FODMAP diet (a diet low in fermentable oligosaccharide, disaccharide, monosaccharide, and polyols) can benefit most people suffering from irritable bowel syndrome (IBS).[673] The low-FODMAP diet is a specific type of elimination diet. An elimination diet removes a food or category of foods that you suspect might negatively affect your health. Some people, for instance, will stop consuming milk (or all dairy products) to see if, after a few weeks, they feel better. Sometimes, people remove many foods at once; then, after a few weeks, if they feel better, they start adding the foods back, one by one, to determine which ones are problematic. Elimination diets have shown benefit for some conditions, such as IBS,[674] but the knowledge they bring isn’t permanent, since, alas, you can become intolerant to f

ermine which ones are problematic. Elimination diets have shown benefit for some conditions, such as IBS,[674] but the knowledge they bring isn’t permanent, since, alas, you can become intolerant to foods that had never before caused you any problem.[675] Q. What are the potential benefits (and risks) of fasting? With regard to weight loss, intermittent fasting (IF) works, but its superiority to regular chronic 351

caloric restriction (CCR) hasn’t been demonstrated.[676][677] So we come back to our mantra: the best diet is the healthy diet you can stick to. Some people find IF diets easier in the long run because of their simplicity: IF diets don’t limit what you can eat; they don’t limit how much you can eat; they only limit when you can eat. Digging Deeper: Different types of intermittent fasting • Fasting Days. You fast or eat very little for 24 hours, either at regular intervals (two days per week in the 5:2 Diet) or just from time to time. Alternate-Day Fasting (below) is a specific type of Fasting Days diet. • Alternate-Day Fasting. You fast every other day. Or you eat over 12 hours then fast for 36 hours. Or, mixing IF with CCR, you eat normally over 24 hours then very little (about 500 Calories) over the next 24 hours. • Random Meal Skipping. You skip meals at random throughout the week. • Feeding Window. You can only eat during a set period of time every day (from 10 a.m. to 6 p.m, for instance). IF can help you lose weight or keep a healthy weight. Does it have other health benefits? Yes. Like CCR, IF has been associated with lower inflammation and with improvements in some biomarke

nstance). IF can help you lose weight or keep a healthy weight. Does it have other health benefits? Yes. Like CCR, IF has been associated with lower inflammation and with improvements in some biomarkers related to healthy aging,[678] and moderate CCR was shown to ameliorate, in the long run, “multiple metabolic and hormonal factors that are implicated in the pathogenesis of type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular diseases, and cancer, the leading causes of morbidity, disability, and mortality”.[679] By kick-starting cellular repair processes in the body, both CCR[680] and IF[681] may reduce the risk of developing age-related diseases.[682] Extended lifespans from CCR and IF have been reported in many animal models[683] (though not all[684]), but keep in mind that those animals were either fed low-calorie diets or rotated through periods of fasting for most of their lives. It is still uncertain if CCR and IF can extend the lifespan of human beings, and if they can, which variant of either type of diet is best and how many weeks, months, or years are required to make a difference. Assessing the potential metabolic benefits of CCR and IF is a long-term endeavor, and as a form of health interv

of diet is best and how many weeks, months, or years are required to make a difference. Assessing the potential metabolic benefits of CCR and IF is a long-term endeavor, and as a form of health intervention, IF is still very new. It may provide unique metabolic benefits over CCR,[682] as well as mood benefits,[685] but solid research is still sparse, so that “further research in 352

humans is needed before the use of fasting as a health intervention can be recommended”.[564] Such caution is warranted, since, while fasting has potential benefits, it also comes with potential risks. Short-term studies on fasting have noted associations with headaches,[686] higher LDL-C cholesterol,[687] greater insulin resistance,[688][689] and reductions in some immune functions.[690] Always make sure to keep hydrated, to reduce the risk of headaches and constipation and avoid many other problems. Can IF hurt your liver? The evidence is mixed. There is animal evidence that fasting can remove toxins from the liver,[691] but also that fasting makes the liver smaller and less efficient.[692] There is also human evidence that CCR can impair the liver’s ability to remove toxins from the body.[693] The longer the fast, the greater the risks, so the Feeding Window diet may be the safest variant of IF. Also, some preliminary evidence suggests that a periodic reduction in caloric intake may produce physiological benefits similar to those of fasting. A fasting-mimicking diet is a strategy where, instead of completely forgoing food, you simply consume a low-calorie diet for 5 days straigh

produce physiological benefits similar to those of fasting. A fasting-mimicking diet is a strategy where, instead of completely forgoing food, you simply consume a low-calorie diet for 5 days straight each month.[694] The typical protocol entails eating 1,090 Calories (10% protein, 56% fat, 34% carb) on the first day, then 725 Calories (9% protein, 44% fat, 47% carb) on each of the next four days.[695] Finally, remember that no diet is everyone’s ideal diet. If you’re pregnant, underweight, younger than 18, or have a history of disordered eating, IF is probably not for you. Q. Is it easier to regain weight if you lost it quickly? According to “common sense” and some government agencies, slow weight loss leads to more sustainable weight loss, yet a 2014 study showed that rapid weight loss can be more effective and doesn’t necessarily lead to more weight regain.[696] Very-low-calorie diets, however, raise safety concerns,[391] less because of the lack of calories than because of the concurrent lack of certain nutrients. When you eat very little, you’re more likely not to get all the vitamins and essential minerals, amino acids, and fatty acids you need to stay healthy. To solve this

current lack of certain nutrients. When you eat very little, you’re more likely not to get all the vitamins and essential minerals, amino acids, and fatty acids you need to stay healthy. To solve this problem, Protein-Sparing Modified Fast (PSMF) diets often use specially designed meal replacements. Early meal replacements used incomplete proteins such as collagen, along with very few micronutrients; fortunately, modern meal replacements rich in high-quality protein and fortified with the right amounts of the right micronutrients have a good safety record when used under medical supervision.[697] All that said, the vast majority of people who need to lose weight can and should do so without resorting to a very-low-calorie diet. If, nevertheless, you consider starting one (whether PSMF or a 353

milder alternative), discuss the topic with your physician first and weigh the risks carefully. Q. How much should you walk while on a diet? In light of the importance of compliance, the cheeky answer to this question is “however much you can stick with”. But let’s look at the findings of a 2002 intervention.[698] Walking on a hypocaloric diet (5 days per week for 12 weeks) WALKING (minutes) 0 30 60 Weight loss (kg / lb) 4.23 / 9.11 5.75 / 12.68 5.85 / 12.90 Fat loss (kg / lb) 2.63 / 5.80 3.53 / 7.78 4.41 / 9.72 Body fat % 1.02 1.2 2.3 Reference: Brill et al. Int J Obes Relat Metab Disord. 2002.[698] According to those results, a 30-minute walk leads to greater weight loss than no walk, and while a 60-minute walk doesn’t lead to greater weight loss than a 30-minute walk, a greater proportion of that loss is fat, hinting at a muscle-sparing effect. The year before this study was published, the American College of Sports Medicine had reviewed a decade’s worth of research and concluded that walking between 150 and 250 minutes per week could improve weight loss (including fat loss) when combined with a moderately calorie-restricted diet.[699] Research supports the health benefits of wa

at walking between 150 and 250 minutes per week could improve weight loss (including fat loss) when combined with a moderately calorie-restricted diet.[699] Research supports the health benefits of walking 10,000 steps each day.[700][701][702] This nice round number is a popular goal, yet is higher than is strictly required, especially for people with illness or injury, or for people whose exercise regimen includes more than walking. And just as tying your self-esteem to a rigid diet goal can be detrimental,[703][704][705][706] so can tying it to a rigid step goal. More than your daily “score”, what matters is your long-term adherence to an 354
exercise regimen, especially since a meta-analysis of diet trials has found that the effect of exercise on weight loss increases with time.[612] 355

The Psychology of Dieting To lose weight, you need to eat less or exercise more — or, for best results, do both.[707] For a 155-pound person, just sixty grams of chocolate-chip cookies, scarfed down in a couple of minutes, are all it takes to offset all the calories burned through half an hour of high-impact aerobics (266 vs. 260 kcal). So if your cravings are out of control, there may not be enough time in a day for you to compensate through exercise. Getting in shape requires continual effort and the will to keep at it when things get tough — which, usually, is near the start, when you begin an exercise regimen and take your first steps toward conquering a bad diet. More than physical, this effort is psychological, especially when it comes to dieting. Exercising requires effort for maybe one hour a day, five days a week, whereas healthy eating requires restraint all day, every day. Worse: you need to keep eating healthy even when your traitorous scale doesn’t show you results the next day, or the next week. Just as you may need to try several kinds of exercise to find one you actually enjoy, you might need to try new things to make healthy eating into a habit. Rather than samplin

t day, or the next week. Just as you may need to try several kinds of exercise to find one you actually enjoy, you might need to try new things to make healthy eating into a habit. Rather than sampling the hot new diet of the month, trying new things means tweaking your diet: new healthy foods; new recipes; different meal frequencies (more frequent, smaller meals, or less frequent, bigger meals); and so on. Too often, people try a fad diet and then, seeing no result after a week, switch to another, and another, and another. Fad diets come and go for a reason — and the reason why they go is that they fail to deliver the near magical results they promise (some diets can, in fact, make you lose a lot of weight in a couple of days, but that weight is mostly water and is easily regained). The main problem with fad diets is that, more often than not, they fail to properly address the root of the problem, which is psychological. A bad diet is seldom due to people not knowing their diet is bad, after all, so simply trying different diets that forbid different foods is unlikely to work. Rather than from nutritional ignorance, a bad diet typically results from whatever food-related thoughts

, after all, so simply trying different diets that forbid different foods is unlikely to work. Rather than from nutritional ignorance, a bad diet typically results from whatever food-related thoughts happen to be floating around in your brain, from stress-induced comfort-food triggers to the guilt you feel from eating too many cookies.[708] The vicious circle of comfort foods 356

Unfortunately, you won’t be able to resolve complex, highly individualized issues through sheer physical effort, or even by reading this. Seeking a dietitian, coach, therapist, or physician might be in order. But right now, in these pages, you can at least note some of the most important pointers leading to successful dieting and weight loss. Common food triggers The modern world is littered with food ads that coax you into satisfying your cravings. While most research is on children, the evidence suggests that adults, too, upon viewing food ads, are likely to overeat.[709] But let’s say you’re immune to blatant ads. Cravings can be shaped by much more subtle influences, from cooking shows[710] to the tempting aromas wafting from restaurants you walk by, to the simple desire to share a good meal with friends. If food triggers were limited to food ads and peer pressure from friends and family, however, controlling your food intake would be merely difficult. But dietary control can seem downright impossible when you incorporate stress-related triggers. Poor sleep or a difficult day at work can too easily make you seek your favorite binge food.[711][712][713] 357
downright impossible when you incorporate stress-related triggers. Poor sleep or a difficult day at work can too easily make you seek your favorite binge food.[711][712][713] 357

How can you deal with all those triggers, which are constantly chipping away at your willpower and dietary control? Sadly, there’s no one-size-fits-all solution. Within the Examine.com staff (which, by the way, consists of flawed humans rather than research robots), strategies vary widely. Some of us allow treats within the confines of a total-calorie daily limit. Others, having honed their habits over years, are able to intuitively eat without much risk of overeating. One of us allows himself sweets only at the end of the day. Another has his partner sequester her treats in an actual lockbox with a combination lock. Yikes! There’s no easy way to tackle this issue. Even if you confine your treats to a lockbox, or simply ban them from your home, if you really want them, you can get them. But this issue can be tackled, in the usual reliable way: practice. To paraphrase a Cherokee tale: You always have two wolves battling within you, one good and one evil. The decisions you make are determined by which wolf is winning. So which wolf will ultimately win? The one you feed. Meaning, the good habit you repeatedly practice will eventually start winning over the bad habit, even when the bad

mined by which wolf is winning. So which wolf will ultimately win? The one you feed. Meaning, the good habit you repeatedly practice will eventually start winning over the bad habit, even when the bad habit is especially hard to break. Tip: Meal planning and batch cooking A good first step to take control of your diet is to make your dietary environment work for you, through meal planning and batch cooking. Meal planning is strongly associated with a healthier diet and less obesity,[714] whereas batch cooking can help you in a couple of ways: Stocking your fridge with healthy, tasty meals gives you options during the week. If this healthy dish doesn’t tempt you right now, maybe that other one will. Cooking meals in advance reduces the mental load of parsing eating options: when you grow hungry, should you snatch a snack, order food, or find the time to cook? No need for any of these — you can thank your earlier self for a delicious meal that only requires heating. Junk food sends seducing signals to your brain[715] and, if consumed in excess, might even change your brain (as it does that of rodents[716]), making healthy foods less pleasurable — so eating junk food is an especially

ing signals to your brain[715] and, if consumed in excess, might even change your brain (as it does that of rodents[716]), making healthy foods less pleasurable — so eating junk food is an especially hard habit to break. Yet breaking it should be your first priority. If you want to lose weight, you may have to both eat healthy and eat less, but healthy eating comes first. Once it has become a habit, you can consider reducing your daily caloric intake. 358

In other words, learn to control your cravings for junk food before you endeavor to control your cravings for food in general. This is especially good advice if you start exercising at the same time you start altering your diet, since restricting your caloric intake right when you start burning more calories can make it hard to exercise and hard to recover. Moreover, after you’ve exercised for a while, you’ll be in a better position to evaluate your maintenance intake (how many calories you need to ingest to neither lose nor gain weight) on exercise days and on rest days. When you start reducing your caloric intake below your maintenance intake, you’ll get hungrier, of course, but for once, time is on your side: if you stick to your calorie-restricted diet for a few months, your cravings should weaken.[717] And in the meantime, some psychological methods may help. A meta-analysis of diet trials found that behavioral support increased weight loss, independently of the type of diet, and that this effect was stronger at 6 than at 12 months[612] (which could be due to the “behavioral support” usually consisting in some sort of counseling at the outset of the study). In particular, acce

that this effect was stronger at 6 than at 12 months[612] (which could be due to the “behavioral support” usually consisting in some sort of counseling at the outset of the study). In particular, acceptance-based methods, which have mindfulness-like components, can reduce your food cravings if you’re highly susceptible to food cues,[718][719][720] though they can also increase your food cravings if your susceptibility to food cues is already low [721] (probably by making you more aware of them). Body image Weeks can pass before fat loss or muscle gain becomes visible, months before you start seeing yourself differently, and years before this new self becomes simply yourself. In short, changing your body takes time, but changing your body image can take even longer. There are two main reasons for that lag: • How you see your body is tied to how you see yourself as a person, and changing how you see yourself as a person is hard. This is a form of self-preservation: we cling to what we consider our self, even when we don’t very much like this self. • What you lose or gain in muscle and fat won’t be visible from one day to the next. Tip: Phototrack your progress Since what you lose or

nsider our self, even when we don’t very much like this self. • What you lose or gain in muscle and fat won’t be visible from one day to the next. Tip: Phototrack your progress Since what you lose or gain in muscle and fat won’t be visible from one day to the next, photographing yourself may prove useful: similar pictures taken weekly or monthly can reveal changes that, looking at yourself daily in the mirror, you couldn’t detect. 359

If you scrutinize your body daily, you’ll convince yourself that you cannot change, that everything you do is useless, a waste of time and effort. So, instead, you need to celebrate small victories (healthier meals, better times or heavier sets at the gym …) and thus change, step by step, how you think about yourself. Never lie to yourself, but focus on the positive, if possible with the support of the people around you. A study showed that teenage girls whose families and friends encourage healthy eating and exercising (positive focus) rather than eating less (negative focus) tend to be more satisfied with their bodies.[722] Like most studies on body image, this one recruited among adolescent females, a group at high risk of negative self-image and thus a frequent target of related interventions; but its conclusions likely apply to adults as well. When you violate a strict diet, it’s easy to get down on yourself for failing.[703][705] In contrast, if you focus on generally healthy habits such as cooking and participating in physical activities you enjoy, you are more flexible[704][706] and thus better able to deal with the occasional overindulgence (such as a holiday feast, an exc

its such as cooking and participating in physical activities you enjoy, you are more flexible[704][706] and thus better able to deal with the occasional overindulgence (such as a holiday feast, an excess of snacks during exams, or the bucket of ice cream you may snarf after a rough breakup). Healthy habits may not seem all that hard to adopt, but consistently practicing them until they really do become habits can be challenging. You may find it easier, in a way, to bend yourself to a strict diet until your resolve buckles under one stressor too many, then binge for a while until willpower or shame brings you back to dieting. Yo-yo dieters, unfortunately, are more likely to have negative body image,[723] just like people who are sick (from notably psoriasis,[724] chronic pain,[725] and inflammatory bowel disease[726]). And if your body image is negative, you are more likely to eat comfort foods, so are more likely to gain weight and develop chronic health conditions that further darken your body image, in a vicious cycle similar to the one that makes you eat cookies to forget your guilt at having eaten too many cookies. Maslow’s hierarchy of (dieting?) needs 360
your body image, in a vicious cycle similar to the one that makes you eat cookies to forget your guilt at having eaten too many cookies. Maslow’s hierarchy of (dieting?) needs 360

According to psychologist Abraham Maslow, you can’t focus on satisfying your higher needs if your more basic needs aren’t already satisfied. And, alas, body image falls under self-esteem, which is toward the top of the pyramid. That means that a lot can take priority over improving your self image — your job, notably, which brings food on the table and helps ensure your safety. Therefore, a looming deadline or some other work-related issue can make it hard for you to actively care for esteem-related issues. Moreover, even if your self-esteem issues are a priority, you can find it hard to decide where to focus your efforts. Should you prioritize your body image or your socioeconomic status? Should you spend more time sweating out at the gym, in an effort to build a better body, or more time slaving away at work, in the hope of getting promoted? 361

But let’s say that, for the time being, improving your body image is your true priority; you might still have problems deciding how to proceed, beyond dieting and exercising. Out of 48 types of interventions, ranging from mindfulness to discussions on self-esteem and healthy habits, none was linked to more than modest improvement in body image.[727] This suggests that body-image issues are too multifaceted for a single intervention to be a cure-all. If a rigorous intervention isn’t enough to solve body-image issues, this paragraph most definitely won’t suffice either. But if you’re reading this, we know one thing about you: you’re an inquisitive person with a desire to change. This inquisitiveness, this desire, can open possibilities, paths to explore. You need to find positive messages that resonate with you, and you need to act on those messages. For example, you can gain some perspective by realizing that many people have self- esteem and body-image issues they never talk about. You are not alone. And you’ve likely overcome challenges in your life that you don’t often think about. You’re better equipped than you think to change your views and habits; it’ll just take consistent p

are not alone. And you’ve likely overcome challenges in your life that you don’t often think about. You’re better equipped than you think to change your views and habits; it’ll just take consistent practice to get to where you want to be. Kamal Patel, Co-founder and Director MBA, MPH, PhD(c) in Nutrition 362

Dieting for Fat Loss Before we get into planning your fat loss strategy, let’s see which general category you fit in: • Seasoned Vet. Some of you have successfully lost fat before and kept it off. Congratulations! It’s hard navigating weight loss when life gets in the way and junk food is everywhere waiting to comfort you. But you might still have a few pounds left to shed to hit your goal, and these tips should help. • Seasoned Yet Disgruntled Vet. You’ve tried different diet and exercise strategies, only to end up at a similar or higher weight. Select your fat-loss combo, follow the guidelines below for a few months, and trust that the results will be different this time. Because this time, you’re basing your fat-loss strategy on best practices informed by the evidence. • Newbie! If you’re just starting out on your fitness journey, expect “newbie” gains: strength and endurance increase rapidly, and weight loss can happen fairly easily. A big problem for people in each category is that plateaus will come, and with them, disappointment. Fitness gains can and will slow to a halt as training experience increases. Why? Because modern life just isn’t conducive to healthy eating or move

t plateaus will come, and with them, disappointment. Fitness gains can and will slow to a halt as training experience increases. Why? Because modern life just isn’t conducive to healthy eating or movement. In the days of our caveman ancestors, you ate all you wanted (when food was available), were forced into an active lifestyle (searching for food and reinforcing your shelter), and maintained a healthy weight most of your life. You never were in a position to watch your figure, and current diseases of excess such as obesity, diabetes, and metabolic syndrome were far and few between. Fast forward to the present day: sedentary lifestyles are the norm and great tasting food is available everywhere. The problem is that our brains’ tendency to seek out calorie-dense foods no longer serves us so well. Moreover, people today can eat at any hour, and no effort is needed to hunt or gather dinner. This evolutionary mismatch has left millions with extra pounds of fat and no easy way to shed it. To increase your chances of success, it’s important to do your pre-diet homework: set a goal, establish a baseline, and decide on a plan. Set a goal Whether your goal is a specific number (e.g., to sh

To increase your chances of success, it’s important to do your pre-diet homework: set a goal, establish a baseline, and decide on a plan. Set a goal Whether your goal is a specific number (e.g., to shed 10 lb / 4.5 kg before a vacation) or a general idea (to be comfortable in your own body), goals can be extremely helpful. 363

At the risk of sounding hokey, chances of success are much lower if you haven’t articulated to yourself what exactly it is that you’re trying to achieve. You may be familiar with the concept of SMART goals. • Specific • Measurable • Achievable • Relevant • Time-bound Not every goal needs to exactly fit those criteria, but be aware of the two torpedoes that most often sink dieting ships: goals that aren’t realistically achievable (i.e., shedding 10 pounds in a week) and goals that aren’t the most relevant (i.e., trying yet another fad diet, when the core issues are really stress and lack of sleep). Establish a baseline A little known fact: studies show that diets ranging from low-carb to low-fat all pretty much work for weight loss — if you stick to them. What really matters is how much you’re eating … and how much less you’re willing to eat. If you are a numbers person, you may find it helpful to get an idea of your total daily energy expenditure (TDEE), which is basically the amount of calories you require to maintain your current body weight. It’s influenced by many things, such as your diet and level of physical activity, but it can serve as a starting point to estimate how much

f calories you require to maintain your current body weight. It’s influenced by many things, such as your diet and level of physical activity, but it can serve as a starting point to estimate how much you need to eat just to keep your current weight. If you take in fewer calories than this, you lose weight. Tip: How do you calculate TDEE? There are many online calculators and equations you can use to estimate your TDEE. Most of them use your height, weight, and age to determine your basal metabolic rate (BMR), which is the amount of calories you’d need every day if you never left your bed. Then, they multiply your BMR by an activity factor (based on how physically active you are) to determine your TDEE. Another way to estimate your TDEE, if your weight is relatively stable, is to start a food 364

journal and keep track of everything you eat and drink for a week, preferably two. Then, calculate how many calories you’ve been consuming and divide the result by the number of days — you’ll find many online tools and smartphone apps to help you in this task (and most will also help you keep track of how much carb, fat, and protein you’re consuming). And if you’re an overachiever, combining the two methods will allow you to compare your theoretical TDEE with what you’ve actually been eating. Decide on a plan OK, so you’ve thought a lot about what you want to achieve, and you have a specific goal, such as getting those abs back before a vacation. You have filled a food log and have an idea of your TDEE. You know where you’re standing. You know where you want to go. Now for the planning phase. Theoretically, any diet will work, as long as you eat under your TDEE (i.e., as long as you ingest fewer calories than you burn). Still, aside from being hypocaloric,[728] the most effective diets tend to share four common traits worth mentioning. • More protein. Whereas the ratio of carb or fat seems to matter little,[612] diets rich in protein consistently lead to greater fat loss (and lesse

tend to share four common traits worth mentioning. • More protein. Whereas the ratio of carb or fat seems to matter little,[612] diets rich in protein consistently lead to greater fat loss (and lesser muscle loss) than diets poor in protein.[729] [730][731][732] Not only is protein more satiating than carb or fat, it is also more thermogenic (your body needs more energy to convert protein into energy).[733][390] • More veggies. Foods rich in water or fiber have low energy density. Because they can easily fill you up, they lead to an involuntary reduction in caloric intake.[734][598] For some 30 kcal, you could eat 1 cup of broccoli … or just ¾ of a teaspoon of coconut oil. The stomach has stretch receptors that sense when it’s getting filled, and they signal the brain that time has come to stop eating.[735] In addition, vegetables are rich in vitamins, minerals, antioxidants, and other healthful compounds — they can help satisfy your body’s needs in micronutrients without overloading it with calories. Comparing two plant-heavy diets 365
it with calories. Comparing two plant-heavy diets 365

• More whole foods. You’ll find it easier to shed fat if you prioritize foods that have been minimally processed.[590][591][592][593] Apples, for instance, which you need to chew even though they’re mostly water, increase satiety and reduce food intake more than applesauce or apple juice do.[596] Also, our body can extract more calories from peanut butter than from whole peanuts, even though both foods have similar caloric values (kcal/g).[736] • More time spent on preparation. Setting time aside to plan and prepare what you’re going to eat over the week is paramount for staying on track with your diet. It can spare you the cumulative stress of deciding what to eat under time pressure (i.e., when meal time comes, each day, several times a day). Also, it allows you to control your portions, which is very hard to do if you eat out.[737] There’s no type of diet that will work best for everyone, but there’s one factor that will decide if you succeed or fail: consistency.[612] You need to choose a diet that fits you — a diet you can stick to. To help you choose, let’s consider a few evidence-backed diets that are also popular among our readers. Three well-researched diets 366
hoose a diet that fits you — a diet you can stick to. To help you choose, let’s consider a few evidence-backed diets that are also popular among our readers. Three well-researched diets 366

Paleolithic Diet The “Paleolithic diet” may not be as popular today as it was just a few years ago, but it still has numerous adherents and, most importantly, a good number of supporting studies. Its goal is to emulate a pre-agricultural hunter-gatherer diet, under the supposition that many of the foods humans began eating in the last 10,000 years have been harmful to our health. While this premise of the Paleo diet can neither be proven nor disproven, there is no denying that the diet itself is rather successful at helping people shed fat and improve their health.[738] Adopting a Paleo diet usually means eating less refined foods and more whole-food proteins and fibrous vegetables — high-satiety foods with low energy density.[739] Moreover, even with similar amounts of calories and similar ratios of macronutrients, the Paleo diet appears to offer some health advantages over other mainstream diets — improved glucose control and lipid profiles, notably.[740] Keto The ketogenic diet was first used in the 1920s as a means to help manage epilepsy in children. It is traditionally a high-fat, low-carb, adequate-protein diet intended to mimic the physiologic state of starvation — without

first used in the 1920s as a means to help manage epilepsy in children. It is traditionally a high-fat, low-carb, adequate-protein diet intended to mimic the physiologic state of starvation — without the detrimental effects of actual starvation. It is still in medical use today, but has also received interest for its potential to increase fat loss compared to diets higher in carbs, based on the simple premise that it increases fat oxidation. However, this does not appear to be the case: given an equal number of calories, ketogenic diets and high-carb diets result in similar fat loss.[613][741] Given an equal number of calories. But for most people, “going keto” means cutting their daily carb intake from hundreds of grams to less than fifty — which usually results in a reduction in total caloric intake, ketogenic diets having been shown to suppress appetite.[742][743] Carbohydrate intake for different diets KETOGENIC LOW-CARB MODERATE- CARB HIGH- CARB AVERAGE US INTAKE <50 g/day 50–150 g/ day 40–65% >65% 49% % = percentage of total daily calories References: Wylie-Rosett et al. Curr Diab Rep. 2013.[744] ● Ford and Dietz. Am J Clin Nutr. 2013.[745] ● Westman et al. Am 367
g/ day 40–65% >65% 49% % = percentage of total daily calories References: Wylie-Rosett et al. Curr Diab Rep. 2013.[744] ● Ford and Dietz. Am J Clin Nutr. 2013.[745] ● Westman et al. Am 367

J Clin Nutr. 2007.[746] It is also important to note that those non-medical ketogenic diets don’t restrict protein to the same extent as their epilepsy-treating counterparts,[747] which is likely one reason they work for weight loss. Keep in mind that fat is the most energy-dense macronutrient. If your fat comes from oils and other processed sources rather than whole foods, don’t be surprised if fat loss doesn’t happen. A ketogenic diet isn’t magic — it just tends to help people eat less. Intermittent Fasting Our bodies are well equipped for long periods of time without food. Generally, a bout of feeding and digestion is followed by an episode of digestive quiescence (i.e., fasting). In modern days, feeding is rather frequent and fasting tends to be limited to our sleep, plus one or two hours on either end. In other words, no more than 8–12 hours for most people. Intermittent fasting (IF) simply extends this fasting period. Fasting can be defined as voluntarily abstaining from food (or from certain types of foods) for a given period of time. IF can take many forms, such as alternate-day fasting (ADF), 5/2 dieting (eating at most 500 kcal on two non-consecutive days each week), and

from certain types of foods) for a given period of time. IF can take many forms, such as alternate-day fasting (ADF), 5/2 dieting (eating at most 500 kcal on two non-consecutive days each week), and time-restricted feeding (TRF; eating only within a set daily time window, which usually lasts 8–12 hours). Ramadan fasting (Ramadan being the Muslim holy month, during which food and drink are consumed only when the sun has set) is a religious form of TRF. Compared to eating normally, TRF has been shown to reduce the body fat of resistance-trained men without affecting their fat-free mass or their strength.[748] Compared to daily caloric restriction, ADF has been shown to produce greater reductions in body fat and lesser reductions in fat-free mass.[749] In addition to its proven efficiency, IF has the advantage of only restricting when (rather than what) you eat, meaning that it can be combined with other dietary approaches. Comparing two meat-heavy diets 368

But about that new diet? Forget about fad diets, especially those claiming to “cleanse” or “detox” your body — they are a marketing gimmick. Those overly restrictive, short-term diets only appear to work because they cause you to drastically cut your calorie intake and pee out a bunch of water, therefore creating the illusion of rapid fat loss. To store 1 g of glycogen in the liver and muscles, the body uses 3 g of water. Glycogen stores are easily depleted in 24–48 hours if the body isn’t getting enough calories or carbohydrates, resulting in a weight loss of several pounds. Once a regular eating schedule is resumed, however, the glycogen and water come rushing back. Nevertheless, the temporary weight loss leads many people to attribute health benefits to the cleanse they’ve just completed. 369

A few parting words as you set on your dieting journey There’s no quick fix or magic pill for achieving your fat loss goals. This Fitness Guide will help ensure your journey is a successful one, but still, Murphy’s Law states that anything that can go wrong will go wrong. You will face adversity in many forms, and there will be setbacks — in the form of illness, injury, or just “falling off the wagon”. More than anything else, your ability to reach any long-term fat loss goal (or any other goal, for that matter) is dependent on your ability to defeat Mr. Murphy. Persistence is Mr. Murphy’s worst enemy. Be confident that whatever obstacles you encounter can be adjusted for, adapted to, and overcome. Kamal Patel, Co-founder and Director MBA, MPH, PhD(c) in Nutrition 370

Movement & Body Positioning No matter how finely tuned your diet, no matter how thorough your workout, your fitness will suffer if you spend the bulk of your day frozen in the same position, with only your eyes and fingers moving much at all. So now, rather than covering the minutiae of gym workouts (e.g., whether three sets is better than two, or the optimal grip for bench press), we’ll be looking at the big picture: when you’re sitting, standing, or moving, what are the most important factors you might not have taken into account? After you’ve read through our review of the evidence, you can always come back to this handy chart to remind yourself of our most important tips on movement and body positioning. Top ten dos and don’ts of movement and body positioning 371

Chair-sitting and ball-sitting Sitting all day is one of the worst health-related habits you can have. Sitting for ≥10 hours a day has been associated with an estimated 34% higher mortality risk.[750] Long, uninterrupted stretches of sitting are especially unhealthy.[751] The longer you sit, the more likely you become to suffer adverse consequences. Because the human head is pretty heavy (9–10 lb / 4.0–4.5 kg), slumping forward can stress your neck muscles and increase the risk of migraines,[752][753] back problems, and “swimmer’s shoulder” (shoulder impingement).[754][755] As for slumping backward (slumping with your butt forward),[756] it can reduce lung capacity and air flow. Finally, whether you slump forward or backward, your stabilizer muscles are underactivated[757] — a situation that can easily spiral into chronic muscular imbalance, leading to injury. Yet sitting isn’t inherently harmful. Toddlers can sit for hours without pain, and compared to adults, they seldom slouch at all. How come? Do toddlers do Pilates? No, they simply haven’t yet developed our bad habits and imbalanced musculature. To reclaim the postural savvy of toddlers (or of hunter-gatherers), you’ll need to

l. How come? Do toddlers do Pilates? No, they simply haven’t yet developed our bad habits and imbalanced musculature. To reclaim the postural savvy of toddlers (or of hunter-gatherers), you’ll need to strengthen your lower back and core muscles through consistent practice. With that goal in mind, people have started sitting on exercise balls (aka stability or Swiss balls) at work, but although ball-sitting does help build a stronger core, it often hurts at first,[758][759] and weeks of it may not even prevent pre-existing back pain from worsening. [760] Of course, as with many pain-related interventions, your mileage may vary. One member of our team who suffered from chronic lower-back pain took up ball-sitting: at first, he could only ball-sit for fifteen minutes, due to middle-back pains; but he increased his ball-sitting time progressively and can now ball-sit for more than one hour before middle-back pains force him to take a break — and his lower-back pain is no more.[761][762][763] Relying on anecdotes isn’t the best option, however. If you suspect that excess sitting factors into your chronic pain, consider seeing a physical therapist or some other movement specialist who ca

3] Relying on anecdotes isn’t the best option, however. If you suspect that excess sitting factors into your chronic pain, consider seeing a physical therapist or some other movement specialist who can tailor an exercise regimen to your specific needs. The past two decades have seen an abundance of research on how best to activate the deep stabilizing muscles — the muscles you most need strengthening to stay pain-free and active. Since the main function of your abs is to keep you stable, you’re more likely to be prescribed planks than crunches, and side planks (aka side bridges) are often used nowadays to more precisely target the oft-neglected transversus abdominis. Not everyone needs physical therapy, but most everyone could benefit from less time spent sitting, especially in uninterrupted stretches. You should try to get up and move for five minutes every half hour.[751][764][765][766][767] You could also try alternating between different positions, some of which 373

we’ll now review. Alternatives to chair-sitting for office work Standing, leaning, kneeling If you can’t get up and move for five minutes per half hour, at least try to stand up for a few minutes now and then, so as to reduce muscular discomfort.[768] With an appropriate work surface, you can even keep working while on your feet: contrary to what you might expect, working standing isn’t likely to affect your reading comprehension or creativity performance, or even your perception of effort or difficulty.[769] Working standing can also make it easier for you to move whenever you can find the time, since you’re already on your feet. You’re not limited to eight minutes, either. Few people with a desk job spend their whole working days standing (even when doing so isn’t impractical or frowned upon by an old-fashioned boss), but more and more people do spend half the day sitting, half the day standing. By spending half the day standing rather than sitting, you could burn upward of 30,000 extra Calories per year, which can translate to several pounds of weight loss.[770] Caution: Standing is healthy — unless you overdo it Standing for too long can, over time, increase your risk of varico

tra Calories per year, which can translate to several pounds of weight loss.[770] Caution: Standing is healthy — unless you overdo it Standing for too long can, over time, increase your risk of varicose veins and lower-body pain. Standing half your working day doesn’t mean standing the whole morning and sitting 374

the whole afternoon, or vice versa: you can alternate as you see fit over the whole day. Also, you should build up your standing time progressively, rather than try to spend half your working day standing from the get-go. Moreover, if you discover that your ideal standing time is less than half your working day, listen to your body. Just like “walking 10,000 steps each day” is a convenient target, so is “standing half the working day” — neither is an ideal target that works for everyone. If you have wonky joints that don’t agree with extended standing, you could try a leaning stool (aka standing stool or standing chair) or a kneeling chair. • A leaning stool keeps your body closer to a standing position (no hinging of the hips) than to a sitting position (hips hinged at around 90 degrees) yet provides enough support to lighten the load on your lower-body joints. • A kneeling chair keeps your body between the standing and sitting positions, as far as your hips are concerned, but puts pressure on your shins. Evidence on kneeling chairs is sparse (with at least one very small study suggesting potential benefit)[771] and evidence on leaning stools is nonexistent, but both implements gi

essure on your shins. Evidence on kneeling chairs is sparse (with at least one very small study suggesting potential benefit)[771] and evidence on leaning stools is nonexistent, but both implements give you options to vary your posture throughout your work day. Squatting Before the invention of office work, people squatted to poop, to work with objects lying on the ground, or simply to take a break from standing. Frequent squatting was just a part of life.[772] Nowadays, when we hear “squatting”, we think of the gym, we think of loaded squats, and while those aren’t harmful[773] (when done with proper technique), we may be missing something by so seldom squatting outside the gym. 375

Digging Deeper: Squatting to get it all out Squatting is the ancestral position for defecation, and squat-to-poop devices have been gaining in popularity over the past few years. With good reason, too: pooping while squatting requires less strain and can be overall healthier.[774] Poop isn’t the only thing that goes out more easily if you squat. Evidence is growing that, to give birth, squatting may be a better position than lying on your back.[775][776][777] It’s hard to recommend butt-to-ground squatting to everyone, though, especially since today’s Americans have knee pains around 50% more often than did their parents.[778] If you have joint pains, you might want to gently work on hip mobility and lower-body flexibility before squatting. If you’re free of pain, you can try squatting a little longer each day; a full squat may eventually become a position in which you, like toddlers, feel comfortable resting. Don’t overdo it, though: injuries or some imbalance in one of the many joints and muscles involved can make squatting for long stretches of time counterproductive. Walking and running Now that we’ve covered sedentary positions, it’s time to address the two major movement cate

s and muscles involved can make squatting for long stretches of time counterproductive. Walking and running Now that we’ve covered sedentary positions, it’s time to address the two major movement categories: walking and running. Hunter-gatherers often walk 6–10 miles a day. Americans? Just 3 on average,[779][780] with wide variations between individuals. Increasing the time you spend walking is a surefire way to improve your health,[781] particularly if the current you is especially sedentary.[782] Walking 10,000 steps a day has been shown to improve blood pressure and other parameters of cardiovascular health.[700][701][702] However, while 10,000 is a nice, round number, and has therefore become a popular goal and one often studied, it is probably not your optimal number. For instance, a study in older women found that, regardless of stepping intensity, mortality rates decreased sharply as steps went up to about 4,400. Beyond that, they decreased more slowly until about 7,500. Beyond that, they stopped decreasing.[783] Dose-response association between mean steps per 376
that, they stopped decreasing.[783] Dose-response association between mean steps per 376

day and all-cause mortality Adapted from Lee et al. JAMA Intern Med. 2019.[783] So if there’s a magic number in this scenario, it is 7,500. Yet even if this happens to be your magical number, it is still just a number. Don’t let it rule you. Do more when you can, do less if you can’t. Don’t decide not to walk at all today because you have time or energy for only 5,000 steps. Don’t feel like you’ve failed today because your fitness tracker says that you’ve walked only 5,000 steps. Stay flexible. What really matters is how much you walk, or move in general, over weeks and months and years — not within a single day. Digging Deeper: A brief history of 377

time spent walking Thanks to motor vehicles, long walks are no longer a necessity of life for most of us. Our ancestors, on the other hand, had no choice but to walk, be it to hunt or gather food. A cheetah can run faster than a gazelle, its favorite prey, whereas a gazelle can run longer than a cheetah. Humans aren’t as fast as either animal, but they can be the best at persistence hunting:[784] their endurance capacity, lack of fur, and ability to sweat allow them to exhaust their prey after hours alternating tracking, walking, running, and waiting. Nowadays, our daily dose of protein is right there in the kitchen; we barely need to walk to reach it. Yet we can still learn from the diverse activities involved in extended hunting. Long periods of varied physical activity can be a fun way to build up endurance, especially when they’re spent outside.[785] So why not take up hiking or, if you’re already in very good shape, trail running? If you carry some extra pounds and feel nervous about starting an exercise regimen, remember: baby steps. If you can’t run, walk. Although running (>6 mph / >9.66 kph) and jogging (≤6 mph / ≤9.66 kph) burn more calories than walking does, the health

out starting an exercise regimen, remember: baby steps. If you can’t run, walk. Although running (>6 mph / >9.66 kph) and jogging (≤6 mph / ≤9.66 kph) burn more calories than walking does, the health benefits are generally similar.[786][787] And if even walking is too much for you in long stretches, take smaller baby steps: start by sitting a little less and standing or leaning a little more (since we spend so much time sitting, there’s plenty of room for improvement there), then spend more time walking as you build up your endurance. Remember: fitness is a journey. Consistency is key; you’re in it for the long haul, and that involves steady, small, gradual improvements, not overexertion and injury. Caution: Listen to your body As you build up your movement routine, be sure to check with your physician in case of persistent aches and pains. Luckily, though, exercise doesn’t appear to play a role in injury patterns of overweight and obese adults.[788] 378
Kamal Patel, Co-founder and Director MBA, MPH, PhD(c) in Nutrition 379

Afterword After reading all this information, you might be wondering: which of these supplements is the most important to take? Banish that thought from your mind! Fitness is a habit, and fitness is cumulative. Supplements can provide a boost, but there’s no ultimate workout or diet or pill. Consistent work and a thirst for knowledge are more powerful than even the most powerful (legal) supplement. Yet we know you can’t keep up with the hundreds of new trials published each year on exercise performance, muscle gain, testosterone optimization, fat loss, cardiovascular health, joint pain, and sleep. So outsource some of that work to us, make sure you understand the most important takeaways, and make the best possible use of your time and efforts. Starting an exercise program is hard. Everyone knows that New Year’s get-in-shape resolutions are seldom kept, and exercise programs are often restarted several times a year. But this time, you’ve got us on your side. Our Fitness Guide will help you. The wealth of information on our website will help you. We can’t exercise for you, or eat healthy in your name, but we can help you focus your efforts and money on what works. Supplements are no

you. The wealth of information on our website will help you. We can’t exercise for you, or eat healthy in your name, but we can help you focus your efforts and money on what works. Supplements are no magic pill, but they can make your journey just a tad easier; they can let you see results just a bit sooner; they can help you persevere until eating healthy and exercising become habits and you realize that, yes, your fitness goals are well within your reach. Now get at it, and keep us updated!. Kamal Patel, Co-founder and Director MBA, MPH, PhD(c) in Nutrition 380
