rine content Smooth surface Tarte taste High synephrine content Lumpy, wrinkled surface BITTER ORANGE GROCERY STORE ORANGE Like ephedrine, synephrine affects the dopamine, epinephrine (a.k.a. adrenaline), and norepinephrine (a.k.a. noradrenaline) systems, causing an increase in metabolic rate. Like ephedrine, synephrine can be used indefinitely without losing

18 Fat Loss — Secondary Options its fat-burning effect, though the feeling of stimulation may fade over time. Like ephedrine, though to a lesser degree, synephrine is a vasoconstrictor (it constricts blood vessels) and thus can interact negatively with monoamine oxidase inhibitors (MAOIs), a kind of antidepressant. Synephrine has only been studied in overweight people — unlike yohimbine, which has also been studied in lean individuals. As a stimulant, synephrine is less potent than yohimbine and seldom taken on its own; it is most often used to complement caffeine, in combination with a white willow bark extract. How to take it Take 10–20 mg twice a day (i.e., 20–40 mg/day). Start at the lower end of the range, especially if you choose to supplement synephrine with other stimulants (or with supplements that potentiate stimulants, such as EGCG). Synephrine should be supplemented on an empty stomach in the morning, and again 6 to 8 hours later. If supplementing synephrine on an empty stomach causes nausea, take it with a meal instead. Synephrine can also be supplemented through a bitter orange extract, in which case said extract should be dosed in accordance with its synephrine conte

mach causes nausea, take it with a meal instead. Synephrine can also be supplemented through a bitter orange extract, in which case said extract should be dosed in accordance with its synephrine content. For example, to supplement 10 mg of synephrine through a bitter orange extract with a 6% synephrine content, take 167 mg of the extract. Bitter orange extracts also contain flavonoids, which may further raise your resting metabolic rate (RMR). However, at least two of those flavonoids (naringenin and hesperidin) also interact with several drug-metabolizing enzymes, which means that bitter orange extracts may interact negatively with pharmaceuticals and thus cannot be recommended over isolated synephrine. Theanine Why it’s a secondary option While theanine (the amino acid L-theanine) does not directly influence fat loss, it can be of use to people supplementing stimulants, notably caffeine. Its calming effect on the nervous system can reduce the overexcitability caused by stimulants without decreasing the stimulant-derived benefits to focus and attention span.
hout decreasing the stimulant-derived benefits to focus and attention span.

19 Fat Loss — Secondary Options In fact, the improvements in concentration induced by caffeine and theanine respectively have been shown to be synergistic. Theanine can be added to any stimulant that provides too much nervous energy at the standard dose. How to take it If you take caffeine, take an equal dose of theanine. At the lower end of the range, if you take 100 mg of caffeine twice a day, take 100 mg of theanine twice a day (i.e., 200 mg/day). At the higher end of the range, if you take 200 mg of caffeine twice a day, take 200 mg of theanine twice a day (i.e., 400 mg/day). White Willow Bark Why it’s a secondary option White willow bark is a plant source of salicin, which is metabolized into salicylic acid, a cousin of acetylsalicylic acid, better known as aspirin. Aspirin in excess is less harmful than salicylic acid in excess, but you need a lower dose of salicin than of aspirin, thanks to synergistic compounds in white willow bark. As it stands, extracts of white willow bark are overall safer than aspirin, which is more likely to damage the lining of the digestive tract. Nevertheless, patients with known aspirin allergy should avoid willow bark products. By inhibiting pros

re overall safer than aspirin, which is more likely to damage the lining of the digestive tract. Nevertheless, patients with known aspirin allergy should avoid willow bark products. By inhibiting prostaglandin production in neurons, salicin can enhance stimulatory signaling and the effects of epinephrine (a.k.a. adrenaline). Salicin is thought to be synergistic with the caffeine-synephrine stack because aspirin has been shown to be synergistic with the caffeine-ephedrine stack (aspirin can potentiate the increase in epinephrine and norepinephrine caused by ephedrine). In short, there is good reason to believe that salicin, and thus white willow bark, can enhance the fat-burning effect of the caffeine-synephrine stack (to a greater extent in people with a higher body fat percentage). White willow bark is only a secondary option because it helps little with fat burning on its own and because it may interact with some drugs — notably blood thinners, such as warfarin (Coumadin). Although to a lesser extent than aspirin, salicin is itself a blood thinner, which is another reason why
o a lesser extent than aspirin, salicin is itself a blood thinner, which is another reason why

20 Fat Loss — Secondary Options white willow bark works well with caffeine and synephrine: It can reduce the strain on the heart caused by these two vasoconstrictors. How to take it Take 60 mg twice a day (i.e., 120 mg/day). A white willow bark extract should be dosed in accordance with its salicin content. For example, to supplement 60 mg of salicin through a white willow bark extract with a 15% salicin content, take 400 mg of the extract. Salicin is most effective when taken with synephrine and caffeine, along with a meal to reduce the possibility of heartburn or upset stomach.

21 Inadvisable Supplements Berberine Berberine is an alkaloid extracted from various plants. It may lower blood levels of sugar and cholesterol, and a few studies found it helped with weight loss. Those studies, however, were of low quality; among other problems, they often did not implement blinding, randomization, or control groups, and they mostly failed to differentiate between weight loss and fat loss. Higher-quality studies attributed no weight or fat loss to berberine. Berberine interacts with metabolic enzymes, and thus with some drugs. Moreover, higher doses (>1 g/day) can cause gastrointestinal upset. For those reasons, the inclusion of berberine in any fat loss stack is not recommended. Green Coffee Extract Green coffee extract (GCE) is an extract of unroasted, green coffee beans, rich in chlorogenic acid. While chlorogenic acid may benefit blood flow, there is limited evidence that it reduces blood sugar levels and none that it helps with fat loss. There is no compelling reason to include it in any fat-burning stack. Figure 5: Green coffee extract: a cautionary tale Poorly conducted trials can lead to exceptional results. It is usually best to wait and see if those resu

ason to include it in any fat-burning stack. Figure 5: Green coffee extract: a cautionary tale Poorly conducted trials can lead to exceptional results. It is usually best to wait and see if those results can be replicated before drawing a conclusion. A study in The Journal of Diabetes, Metabolic Syndrome, and Obesity claims that the participants taking GCE lost about 1.5 lb/month. 1 The original study is retracted. Trial data could not be independently verified for accuracy. 4 Millions of dollars’ worth of GCE sells in the following years. 3 Dr. Oz begins touting GCE as a “miracle weight loss pill”. 2

22 Fat Loss — Inadvisable Supplements Fucoxanthin One human study showed benefit from 2.4–8 mg/day, but it used a proprietary seaweed extract, has never been replicated, and its results seem too good to be true: an average fat loss of 3.6 kg (7.9 lb) in 16 weeks. More research is needed before fucoxanthin can be recommended for fat loss. Hoodia Gordonii According to preliminary animal evidence, a bioactive compound in Hoodia gordonii (an African cactus) could suppress appetite, but follow-up research has confirmed that oral supplementation does not deliver this bioactive to the brain. Moreover, hoodia supplementation is associated with substantial blood pressure increases and minor but concerning increases in heart rate and liver enzymes. There is no evidence that Hoodia gordonii can help with fat loss, but plenty that it can damage the liver and the cardiovascular system. Hoodia gordonii should not be included in any stack. Raspberry Ketone Raspberry ketone is a molecule initially extracted from raspberries but now mass-produced synthetically for use in perfumes and cosmetics. Early evidence suggested that raspberry ketones could promote fat oxidation, but the ketone concentration

racted from raspberries but now mass-produced synthetically for use in perfumes and cosmetics. Early evidence suggested that raspberry ketones could promote fat oxidation, but the ketone concentration required for this effect is too high to be achieved through oral supplementation. The original fat loss claim stems from toxicity studies in animals, not from fat loss studies in humans, and the one fat loss study in humans that showed benefit was highly confounded: The product tested contained not only raspberry ketones but also caffeine, capsaicin, garlic, ginger, and bitter orange as a source of synephrine, making it impossible to assess the respective efficacy of each ingredient. In short, current evidence does not support the inclusion of raspberry ketones in any fat loss stack.

23 Fat Loss — Inadvisable Supplements Senna Alexandrina Senna alexandrina, or just senna, is an herb frequently marketed as a dietary cleanser and detoxifier. However, while it can cause temporary weight loss thanks to its potent laxative effect, it lacks an inherent fat loss effect. Daily or chronic use of any potent laxative (except for fiber and caffeine) can result in colon damage. Senna alexandrina should not be included in any stack.

24 Assembling Your Stack Incorporating Core Supplements Consume 1.5–2.2 grams of protein per kilogram of body weight over the course of the day (0.68–1.00 g/lb/day), with the help of protein powders if necessary. Keep at it for at least one month before making any modification, such as adding one of the following options. Incorporating Options Combining supplements should always be done cautiously, but people combining stimulants should be especially careful. Stimulants can be synergistic; when combined, even low doses can have powerful effects … and be potentially dangerous, notably for the heart. To burn fat without stimulants Take 5-HTP (300 mg/day, increasing to 500 mg/day over a week), EGCG (400–500 mg/day), fiber (consult the dedicated entry to set your intake), and MCTs (2–12 g/day). Once in a while, suspend supplementation of 5-HTP to assess if it is still able to reduce your carbohydrate cravings. This stack can be supplemented year round, on its own or in conjunction with the stimulant stack: To burn fat with stimulants Take the following doses twice a day for 12 weeks, followed by a 12-week break. Take 100–200 mg of caffeine. Once tolerance sets in, add synephrine (10–20

stimulant stack: To burn fat with stimulants Take the following doses twice a day for 12 weeks, followed by a 12-week break. Take 100–200 mg of caffeine. Once tolerance sets in, add synephrine (10–20 mg) and a white willow bark extract (60 mg of salicin). For both caffeine and synephrine, start at the lower end of the range.

25 Fat Loss — Assembling Your Stack If, for your next 12-week cycle, you wish for greater stimulation, add yohimbine (2.5 mg, increasing to 5 mg after a week and 7.5 mg after another week). Once accustomed to this protocol, you can add forskolin (25 mg). Other options Adding theanine to an equal dose of caffeine can ward off overstimulation while retaining the benefits to attention span and focus. If you take 100– 200 mg of caffeine twice a day, take 100–200 mg of theanine twice a day. Alternatively, you could drink several cups of black tea (the type of tea with the highest amount of caffeine). Caffeine can be consumed as pills but also through coffee, tea, energy drinks, etc. You could try replacing 200 mg of caffeine by either 50–75 mg of guarana (assuming a 9% caffeine content) or 1 g of yerba mate leaf powder (about 17.5 mg of caffeine), but neither option is recommended, and both should be avoided if you take amphetamines (e.g., Adderall). Synephrine (10–20 mg) can be replaced by a bitter orange extract (167–333 mg, assuming a 6% synephrine content), with the understanding that some of the flavonoids in bitter orange may interact negatively with pharmaceuticals. Salicin can b

a bitter orange extract (167–333 mg, assuming a 6% synephrine content), with the understanding that some of the flavonoids in bitter orange may interact negatively with pharmaceuticals. Salicin can be replaced by a baby aspirin (81 mg). The warnings in the White Willow Bark entry also apply to aspirin.

26 FAQ Can I add to my stack a supplement not covered in this guide? Supplement your current stack for a few weeks before attempting any change. Talk to your doctor and research each potential new addition in advance. Check for known negative interactions with other supplements in your current stack, but also for synergies. If two supplements are synergistic or additive in their effects, you might want to use lower doses for each. Can I modify the recommended doses? If a supplement has a recommended dosage range, stay within that range. If a supplement has a precise recommended dose, stay within 10% of that dose. Taking more than recommended could be counterproductive or even dangerous. Should I take my supplements with or without food? And at what time? Answers are provided in each supplement entry whenever the evidence permits. Too often, however, the evidence is either mixed or absent. Besides, a supplement’s digestion, absorption, and metabolism can be affected differently by different foods. Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K), for instance, are better absorbed with a small meal containing fat than with a large meal containing little to no fat. Starting with half the regular dos

rent foods. Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K), for instance, are better absorbed with a small meal containing fat than with a large meal containing little to no fat. Starting with half the regular dose can help minimize the harm a supplement may cause when taken during the day (e.g., tiredness) or in the evening (e.g., insomnia). Should I continue using white willow bark if it aggravates my heart burn? No, you shouldn’t. Although a study reported that, “in contrast to synthetic aspirin, willow bark [did] not damage the gastrointestinal mucosa” (probably thanks to some phenolic compounds in the plant), there is still a risk of it exacerbating stomach ulcers. Keep in mind that your body metabolizes salicin into salicylic acid, a close cousin of acetylsalicylic acid (aspirin), which several studies have shown can damage the lining of the digestive tract. Why isn’t ephedrine included in this guide? Ephedrine, an alkaloid from the plant Ephedra sinica, cannot be marketed as a

27 Fat Loss — FAQ fat burner. Its sale is prohibited or restricted in various countries, so it cannot be recommended in this guide. Synephrine is similar to ephedrine. It is a safer compound, though it is also less effective. Why isn’t DNP included in this guide? DNP (2,4-dinitrophenol) is most often sold as an herbicide or a pesticide; it cannot legally be sold as a food supplement. Ingested, it creates a leak in the energy pathway of mitochondria, the ATP generators in our cells, so that everything you do, from exercising down to breathing, burns more calories (as heat). For that reason, DNP has been studied as a potential obesity drug, and it has proven very effective. Alas, the side effects include dysgeusia (a distortion of the sense of taste), cataracts, and death. Because the efficacious dose and the lethal dose are very close and vary between individuals, even what was once considered a safe dosage has killed. Since our bodies all react differently, and since the line between the efficacy and lethality of DNP is thin, what was safe for your friend may not be safe for you. At any dose, taking DNP is very much like playing Russian roulette.
he efficacy and lethality of DNP is thin, what was safe for your friend may not be safe for you. At any dose, taking DNP is very much like playing Russian roulette.

28 Precautions and Troubleshooting Stack components are seldom studied together. The safest way to add supplements to your daily routine is one at a time, at least a couple of weeks apart, to better assess the effects (and side effects) of each new addition. Start at half the regular dose for a week, then slowly increase to the regular dose if you are not experiencing the desired effects. Any supplement that can affect the brain, especially supplements with a stimulatory or sedative effect, should first be taken in a controlled situation. Do not take a dose, least of all your first dose, before events such as driving or operating heavy machinery, when impaired cognition may be a risk for your safety and the safety of others. Most stimulants can disrupt sleep and should not be supplemented in the evening. If sleep does not come easily or if you sleep badly several nights in a row, suspend supplementation. Using a fat burner is counterproductive if sleep quality suffers.

Tanks for buying the Examine Fitness Guide: A blueprint for optimizing nutrition and supplemen- tation. As a special thank-you bonus, we’re including this set of ﬁtness-related articles from another Examine product: the Nutrition Examination Research Digest (NERD). Tese articles provide a diﬀerent perspective than the Fitness Guide. Te guide’s goal is to give you an overview of the best evidence on all six facets of ﬁtness, from exercise and nutrition to understanding hormone regulation and joint health. You’ll explore diﬀerent dietary approaches that are both sustainable and compatible with your lifestyle, as well as learn about how the diﬀerent aspects of ﬁtness are intercon- nected. Ten, we review the most eﬀective strategies for optimizing each of those facets. Te guide’s aim is to equip you with the evidence-based tools you need to achieve your personal ﬁtness goals through smart, intentional action. In short, you’ll get what’s on the label: a holistic, no-hype guide to ﬁtness! NERD is not dedicated to a single topic. Instead, the editors dive deep into the latest research coming out of the world of health and nutrition. We scour the literature from the past few months and cho

RD is not dedicated to a single topic. Instead, the editors dive deep into the latest research coming out of the world of health and nutrition. We scour the literature from the past few months and choose articles based on a variety of factors: Are the contents making the news? Does the research make an important contribution to nutrition science? And most importantly: Does the study include information that our readers will ﬁnd useful and interesting? Over the past several years, interest in several methodologies for improving health and ﬁtness — from high-intensity interval training to intermittent fasting and the ketogenic diet — has grown massively among both health enthusiasts and scientists. For example, a quick search of PubMed reveals over 3,000 scientiﬁc articles on the ketogenic diet, with around half of them published in the past ﬁve years alone. Not only is there more information than ever before, but people are increasingly spending more money on products purported to improve their health. Te dietary supplements industry in the U.S. captured over $35 billion of market share in 2020 and continues to grow by millions of dollars per year. With so much information and so ma

ve their health. Te dietary supplements industry in the U.S. captured over $35 billion of market share in 2020 and continues to grow by millions of dollars per year. With so much information and so many options to sif through, NERD has kept pace by covering some of the most interesting health-related studies each month and distilling them into digestible, actionable take- aways for readers. Te present collection includes eight articles that we published in 2020, investigating the most cutting edge lines of questioning in supplementation and exercise recovery research, as well as an exclusive interview with a leading researcher in the ﬁeld of sports nutrition. We recommend you take some time to check them out because we really think they serve as a great complement to the Fitness Guide. Letter from the editor (cont)

Letter from the editor (cont) Tat’s because NERD covers some domain-speciﬁc nitty-gritty details relevant to each cutting-edge study we review. For example, you’ll learn about the motivation behind each study: In “Supercharging muscle protein synthesis through essential amino acids,” we start by discussing the mechanism powering muscle protein synthesis and its importance for health and ﬁtness outcomes. As you read, you’ll pick up tips about how to interpret studies on your own by reviewing the methods the researchers used, their reasoning for their choices, and our own critiques of those methods. In short, this collection provides a more thorough understanding of the research at hand and helps you both with your ﬁtness toolset, and your future adventures in the world of health and nutrition research. I hope you enjoy the journey! Gregory Lopez, Editor-in-Chief

The relationship between vitamin C supplementation and exercise remains unclear Tags: Vitamin C, Exercise Study under review: Effects of vitamin C on oxidative stress, inflammation, muscle soreness, and strength following acute exercise: meta-analyses of randomized clinical trials. Quick Takes • What question this study addressed: *How does vitamin C supplementation affect markers of oxidative stress, inflammation, and muscle soreness and strength after acute exercise? • How the question was answered: Through meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials. • Who was studied: Mostly healthy, active men who weren’t athletes, although some athletes and women were involved in a handful of the trials. • What the intervention was: 400 milligrams to 3 grams of vitamin C, ranging from one day to a month of supplementation, followed by mostly aerobic exercise. • What the main takeaway is: Vitamin C supplementation had 4

no clear effect on any measured parameter shortly after the exercise session, except for one controversial measure of oxidative stress (lipid peroxidation via TBARS) and one measure of inflammation (IL-6). • Caveats: There were a wide variety of trials measuring many outcomes with small numbers of participants, suggesting that there’s a lot more room for more and better quality evidence. What was the question? Does vitamin C supplementation affect oxidative stress, inflammation, muscle soreness, or strength following a single bout of aerobic or anaerobic exercise? Why was the question worth asking? The beneficial health effects observed after performing regular exercise are due to the adaptations that occur in several systems in the body, like the cardiovascular and musculoskeletal systems. The specific types of adaptations depend on the exercise modality. In skeletal muscle, exercise can increase inflammatory processes and oxidative stress acutely, which in turn trigger compensatory coping mechanisms. For instance, exercise has been shown[1] to increase the production of reactive oxygen and nitrogen species, which serve as signaling molecules and increase the endogenous antioxidan

oping mechanisms. For instance, exercise has been shown[1] to increase the production of reactive oxygen and nitrogen species, which serve as signaling molecules and increase the endogenous antioxidant capacity in the long term. The acute response to exercise, while important [2] for long term adaptations, is also accompanied by reduced function[3] of the muscle (i.e. decline in strength) and muscle pain or soreness caused by muscle damage. This is inconvenient for both regular people who want to exercise, as it can prevent people from continuing to exercise due to pain, or for athletes concerned about performance after exercise. Vitamin C is a very popular supplement that can act as an antioxidant, and should, in theory, be able to reduce exercise-induced oxidative stress and inflammation. However, research on its effects on exercise-induced inflammation and oxidative stress, and related functional metrics, is still inconclusive. 5

Acutely, exercise increases inflammation and induces oxidative stress, which can cause a reduction in muscle strength and increase muscle soreness. This might be inconvenient for people who want to exercise frequently or athletes who need to maintain high performance after an exercise bout. Vitamin C could help attenuate this response, but the results to date are inconclusive. How was the question answered? Researchers conducted a random-effects meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials (RCT) following the PRISMA guidelines and Cochrane Handbook. The trial was registered in PROSPERO. The study included data from 18 RCTs, with a total of 313 participants with a median age of 24 years. Of the 313 participants, 72% were men and only 13% were athletes. The others were described as active and healthy participants by the authors. The dose of vitamin C that was administered ranged from 400 milligrams per day to 3 grams per day, consumed orally, and aerobic exercise (i.e. running or cycling) was the main exercise modality (15 RCTs). Most studies (N=12) used a supplementation scheme for more than a week. The longest supplementation protocol lasted for 28 days. Lipid peroxidation (using

g) was the main exercise modality (15 RCTs). Most studies (N=12) used a supplementation scheme for more than a week. The longest supplementation protocol lasted for 28 days. Lipid peroxidation (using MDA and TBARS) was included in the meta-analysis as a measure of oxidative stress, and inflammatory markers included interleukin 6 (IL-6) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Muscle damage was assessed using creatine kinase (CK) levels. Cortisol, muscle soreness and muscle strength, as a means to quantify performance detriments, were also included in the analysis. The current meta-analysis included 313 healthy young participants (72%) supplementing with 400 milligrams per day to 3 grams per day of vitamin C. Most trials used aerobic exercise as the exercise modality and only 13% were conducted in athletes. The levels of lipid peroxidation, IL-6, CRP, CK, cortisol and muscle soreness and strength in response to vitamin C supplementation were analyzed. 6

What was the answer? As shown in Figure 1, there was a statistically significant, but small in magnitude, reduction in lipid peroxidation acutely after exercise (up to two hours), but not over longer time periods. Subgroup analyses showed that the effect was significant when doses of vitamin C were higher than 500 milligrams, when exercise intensity was high and when lipid peroxidation was assessed using TBARS. Figure 1: Effects of vitamin C on oxidative stress and inflammation markers up to two hours post-exercise (with 95% confidence intervals) Similarly, vitamin C supplementation caused a small reduction in the levels of IL-6 only in the immediate two hours after exercise. On the other hand, vitamin C had no effect on CRP, muscle damage, muscle soreness, or muscle strength. 7

Overall, the risk of bias for most studies was unclear, and the quality of evidence (using GRADE) for lipid peroxidation and IL-6 up to two hours after exercise was moderate, although very low for lipid peroxidation after two hours. Vitamin C supplementation lowered one measure of oxidative stress and one metric of inflammation two hours after exercise. No clear effects on muscle damage markers, muscle soreness, or muscle strength were observed. How much should you trust the answer? Overall, vitamin C showed no effect on most variables, and only a moderate effect on lipid peroxidation (assessed by TBARS) and IL-6, acutely after exercise. The very short lived effect of vitamin C supplementation agrees with its oral bioavailability: after ingestion, peak levels occur after about 40 minutes[4]. This is why supplement timing might be of pivotal importance to elicit an effect. However, corresponding information about when the supplement was taken in the included studies was not consistently available. Another important consideration is that lipid peroxidation was only significantly reduced when assayed using TBARS, which has been shown to be flawed[5] and is currently not recommended, e

available. Another important consideration is that lipid peroxidation was only significantly reduced when assayed using TBARS, which has been shown to be flawed[5] and is currently not recommended, especially in exercise contexts. The main limitation of this meta-analysis is the very low number of RCTs that were available for analysis. For example, there were very few studies that analyzed other, more reliable markers of oxidative stress and thus couldn’t be included in the analysis. Data for IL-6 also comes from only six RCTs, and the number of RCTs that tested muscle damage (N=9), soreness (N=7) and strength (N=5) was also very low. Moreover, the low number and heterogeneity of data precluded the analysis of differences by dosage, timing of supplementation, exercise modalities, or populations. The same is true for differences between athletes and recreationally active participants (given that the exercise sessions might be more demanding), or with resistance instead of endurance exercise, for which the acute inflammatory response appears to be important[6] for hypertrophy and strength adaptations. Vitamin C supplementation only had a minor effect lasting for a short period of ti

rcise, for which the acute inflammatory response appears to be important[6] for hypertrophy and strength adaptations. Vitamin C supplementation only had a minor effect lasting for a short period of time after an exercise session, which agrees with 8

its short bioavailability. The reduction in lipid peroxidation was only significant when it was determined using a technique which is not recommended due to methodological problems. Overall, the number of RCTs included in the meta-analysis was too low for some parameters of interest to be evaluated conclusively. What’s the take-home? Vitamin C supplementation might moderately reduce oxidative stress and inflammation only acutely after exercise, but doesn’t appear to affect measures of muscle damage, soreness, or strength. As the reduction in oxidative stress was observed only after researchers used a technique that is not recommended and because there was a low number of trials included in the meta-analysis, extreme caution is warranted when it comes to the interpretation of the results. This is all the more true for generalizations or predictions of long(er) term effects on adaptation. Future and larger studies are needed to properly evaluate the effects of vitamin C supplementation. Adding protein to carbohydrates for better endurance performance Tags: CHO, Protein, Endurance Training Study under review: The Effect of Ingesting Carbohydrate and Proteins on Athletic Performance: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of Randomized Controlled Trials Quick Takes • What question this study addressed: Does adding protein to carbohydrates improve athletic performance and reduce exhaustion? • How the question was answered: Researchers addressed the question through a meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials. • Who was studied: The participants were mostly active men who liked to cycle. • What the intervention was: Researchers compared carbohydrate and carbohydrate-protein in a variety of ratios, with the most common being 4:1. Timing of the doses also varied between trials. • What the main takeaway is: Protein-carbohydrate improved time trial performance and time to exhaustion more than carbohydrate alone. 11

• Caveats: The experimental and supplementation protocols varied widely between trials. Consistent performance benefits were observed only when treatments were standardized for carbohydrate intake, meaning when the CHO+PRO group consumed more calories. What was the question? Do carbohydrates combined with protein increase endurance performance compared to just carbohydrates in exercise time trials or time to exhaustion tests? Why was the question worth asking? The general recommendation for endurance athletes is to consume 5–12 grams of carbohydrates[1] per kilogram of bodyweight per day. The suggested intake depends on the amount of exercise per day and proximity of an exercise event[2], though recommendations vary among governing bodies. These recommendations come from a variety of sources that have been updated as new evidence is published, although they haven’t changed much in the past decade. After general recommendations are met, athletes often look to gain marginal benefits by taking supplements or altering their nutrition timing[3]. Carbohydrates are stored as glycogen in the liver and skeletal muscle. The rationale for consuming carbohydrates around the time of training is

aking supplements or altering their nutrition timing[3]. Carbohydrates are stored as glycogen in the liver and skeletal muscle. The rationale for consuming carbohydrates around the time of training is that they can be used more quickly for energy than fat through circulating blood glucose or glycolysis[4]. There is also a rationale for consuming carbohydrates after training because glycogen depletion is associated with fatigue, reduced work capacity, and impaired cognitive function[5], so athletes who compete in multiple events may need to refuel quickly. The exact mechanism of fatigue is unknown[6] but may be due, in part, to the depletion of glycogen, which results in a reduction in the rate of ATP regeneration[6]. Protein plays an important role in synthesizing new proteins and repairing muscle damage[7]. Endurance athletes often focus less on protein consumption than athletes who resistance train because they don’t need as much muscle mass. Recommendations for protein intake by endurance athletes range from 1.2–1.4 grams[8] per kilogram of bodyweight per day. It is also 12
urance athletes range from 1.2–1.4 grams[8] per kilogram of bodyweight per day. It is also 12

recommended that endurance athletes consume protein around their workout to enhance recovery and performance[9]. The combination of protein and carbohydrates has been suggested to improve performance in various studies by reducing muscle damage[10] or resynthesizing glycogen faster[11]. The goal of this study was to determine if the combination of protein and carbohydrates could improve performance compared to carbohydrates alone and, if this effect occurs, when the optimal time is to consume such a supplement. Carbohydrate recommendations for endurance athletes range from 5–12 grams per kilogram of bodyweight per day, and there may be ways to improve performance or recovery around training sessions by combining carbohydrate and protein intake. How was the question answered? This study was a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials on mid- or post-workout supplementation. Studies were included if they investigated the effect of the co- ingestion of carbohydrates and proteins (CHO-PRO) compared to only carbohydrates (CHO) in healthy adults. Studies with short-term (less than eight hours) and long-term (more than eight hours) recovery periods were included

tes and proteins (CHO-PRO) compared to only carbohydrates (CHO) in healthy adults. Studies with short-term (less than eight hours) and long-term (more than eight hours) recovery periods were included with data on time to exhaustion or time trials. A time trial is when athletes are given a certain distance to cover and their completion time is recorded. Time to exhaustion is when athletes are told to exercise at a certain rate, generally a percentage of VO2max, for as long as possible. Researchers completed sub-analyses to determine the effect ingestion timing and calorie content during time to exhaustion. Studies were also analyzed for risk of bias using the Cochrane Collaboration’s risk-of-bias tool[12], which includes details on the risk of bias from six domains: generation of the allocation sequence, concealment of the allocation sequence, blinding, incomplete outcome data, selective outcome reporting, and biases arising from randomization, deviations from the intervention, missing data, measurements, and selective reporting. This study was not preregistered. The meta-analysis under review analyzed randomized controlled 13
data, measurements, and selective reporting. This study was not preregistered. The meta-analysis under review analyzed randomized controlled 13

trials that investigated carbohydrate ingestion or carbohydrate combined with protein ingestion during endurance exercise. The main outcomes were time to exhaustion or time-trial performance. Sub-analyses were conducted to explore short vs. long-term recovery periods, and caloric content. A risk-of-bias tool was used. The study was not preregistered. What was the answer? The authors identified 30 studies with 326 participants total, but only 11 people on average per study. The vast majority of the participants were male and all but one used a cross-over design. A cross-over design uses the same participants in both (CHO and CHO-PRO) groups. The strength of this design is that participants are their own controls, so confounding is lower if an appropriate wash-out period is used[13]. Studies included data from participants who exercised on bikes or treadmills. Figure 1: Overall results 14

This meta-analysis included 24 studies that compared the effect of CHO-PRO to CHO for time to exhaustion. As shown in Figure 1, the authors found a significant effect in favor of CHO-PRO, which equated to being able to exercise for 3.62 minutes longer. The heterogeneity in these studies was low, but the confidence intervals were large, indicating that the true size of the effect is unclear. The overall analysis on time trial performance favored the CHO-PRO group and indicated a 1.5 minute benefit. No sub-analysis was completed on time trials. The authors then tried to determine if the timing of CHO-PRO ingestion played a role, so nine trials were analyzed that compared the effects of CHO-PRO to CHO during time to exhaustion tests. The authors found a significant effect in favor of CHO-PRO, which equated to exercising for 8.53 minutes longer. However, the 14 studies that analyzed the effect of CHO-PRO vs. CHO during the recovery period indicated no significant effect. This means that the timing of ingestion plays a role in the overall comparison, and taking CHO-PRO during exercise rather than after may provide more benefits. The authors also completed a sub-analysis and found no eff

e timing of ingestion plays a role in the overall comparison, and taking CHO-PRO during exercise rather than after may provide more benefits. The authors also completed a sub-analysis and found no effect of CHO-PRO vs. CHO on short- term recovery (nore more than eight hours). They found a significant long-term effect (more than eight hours) equating to an additional 10.59 minutes in the time to exhaustion test. Two of the studies analyzed during the long-term sub-analysis used CHO or CHO-PRO, during and after the exercise bout, which could explain why the sub-analysis was significant since there were only three studies total. This, again, indicates that ingestion during exercise is key. The authors also completed a sub-analysis of non-isocaloric CHO-PRO vs. CHO on time to 15

exhaustion using 12 studies. They found a significant effect in favor of CHO-PRO by 3.99 minutes. This helps tease out whether calories matter, rather than macro content alone. In another sub- analysis, the authors analyzed six carbohydrate matched studies and found a significant effect of CHO-PRO of 8.63 minutes. This implies that when carbohydrates were matched, protein played a major role in performance. The main takeaways from the subgroup analyses are depicted in Figure 2. Figure 2: Subgroup analyses for time to exhaustion 16

The risk of bias assessment indicated that almost all studies had an unclear risk of random sequence generation, some had an unclear risk of selection bias or other bias, all studies had an unclear risk of detection bias (blinding of outcome), and most studies had a low risk of attrition or reporting bias. Overall, only six studies had a high risk of bias, each in only a single category, so these studies mostly were mostly well-designed. CHO-PRO improves performance on time to exhaustion and in time trials compared to CHO alone. Sub-analysis indicates this is likely due to the consumption of CHO-PRO during exercise and may be due to the added calories of CHO-PRO, compared to CHO. How much should you trust the answer? The ingestion of carbohydrates and protein, together or independently, has been well studied to determine if it can improve endurance performance. Several studies indicate carbohydrates alone can improve performance directly by elevating blood glucose and glycogen resynthesis[14]. Protein intake can help increase muscle size and repair muscle damage. Adding protein to carbohydrates potentially increases amino acid availability, increases insulin response, and potential

sis[14]. Protein intake can help increase muscle size and repair muscle damage. Adding protein to carbohydrates potentially increases amino acid availability, increases insulin response, and potentially decreases cortisol levels[15]. Thus, the authors hoped to harmonize the literature by using a meta-analysis to compare the effects of CHO-PRO vs. CHO on endurance performance. The current study used time trials and time to exhaustion tests. Since there are large individual differences[16] in the variability of time to exhaustion during submaximal exercise, using a meta- analysis to pool smaller studies together helps researchers understand the body of literature. Exhaustion trials are reproducible in trained cyclists[17] and runners[18] if they are exercising at the same relative intensity. However, there seem to be differences in the responses between trained and untrained participants[19], in that trained participants adapt to higher intensity faster. The current study used data from participants with training backgrounds ranging from recreational athletes to elite cyclists. Furthermore, time to exhaustion tests allow for greater variability between studies because of the possible

participants with training backgrounds ranging from recreational athletes to elite cyclists. Furthermore, time to exhaustion tests allow for greater variability between studies because of the possible differences between fitness levels, which could allow for greater exercise time. For example, in Karp et al.[20], participants cycled for 25–40 minutes depending on the group, while in Saunders et al.[21], participants cycled for more than 100 minutes. The data was likely too small to conduct a sub-analysis for training status, so whether it plays a role is unclear. 18

The authors found that there was a benefit for CHO-PRO supplementation in non-isocaloric studies. This could be partially due to the increase in total calories when protein is consumed in addition to carbohydrates. A systematic review and meta-analysis[15] by Stearns et al. found there was no significant improvement in cycling time to exhaustion with protein supplementation when compared to either an iso-carbohydrate or iso-caloric drink. In support of the current results, Stearns et al. also found that co-ingestion of protein with carbohydrate during exercise improved performance by about 9%, compared to carbohydrate alone. It would have been illuminating to review an overall isocaloric analysis in the current study. The amount of muscle glycogen depletion determines, to a large extent, its rate of resynthesis[22], so protocols that depleted more glycogen in the studies in this meta-analysis could influence the amount of glycogen replenished and thus the time trial or exhaustion outcome. The intensity of exercise in the studies analyzed ranged from 70–85% VO2max and the time trials ranged from 5–60 kilometers during the follow-up test. To give some context, a depletion protocol of

me. The intensity of exercise in the studies analyzed ranged from 70–85% VO2max and the time trials ranged from 5–60 kilometers during the follow-up test. To give some context, a depletion protocol of 70% VO2max for about 50 minutes caused a 50% decline in muscle glycogen. The rate of glycogen resynthesis begins after exercise and is highest during approximately the first six hours of recovery[23], which is why the authors analyzed early (no more than eight hours) and late recovery (more than eight hours). Recommendations for post-exercise carbohydrate consumption are to consume 0.6–1.0 grams per kilogram[9] of bodyweight within 30 minutes of finishing and every two hours for the next six hours[9]. However, the relative carbohydrate intake of the participants was not provided. In cases of suboptimal post-exercise carbohydrate consumption, the addition of protein can improve glycogen replenishment and decrease symptoms of muscle damage[9], so the relative amount could play a large role. Even small differences in nutrition can have a significant effect on performance for athletes. For example, the difference between finishing first and second in the men’s marathon at the 2016 Olympic

Even small differences in nutrition can have a significant effect on performance for athletes. For example, the difference between finishing first and second in the men’s marathon at the 2016 Olympics in Rio was about 1%. Endurance athletes are often at risk of underperformance syndrome[24], so recovery and performance must be adapted accordingly. If CHO-PRO helps tip the scale in favor of better performance, then it may be a worthwhile protocol for helping athletes train consistently while remaining healthy. Carbohydrate plus protein consumption improves time to exhaustion and time trial performance in endurance athletes, but only in the short-term (no more than eight hours). This may be due to elevated glycogen resynthesis from the added protein. 19

What’s the take-home? There may be a marginal benefit from consuming carbohydrates in combination with protein, compared to carbohydrates alone, for improving endurance performance. This effect appears to occur when the nutrients are taken during or after exercise. This effect could be the result of the added calories from the additional protein, or the combination of carbohydrates, protein, and increased calories. The current study included a wide range of study protocols, and the participants had varied training statuses, which make the data difficult to fully interpret. D-ribose for diminishing DOMS Tags: Muscle Soreness, D-Ribose Study under review: Effect of D-ribose supplementation on delayed onset muscle soreness induced by plyometric exercise in college students Quick Takes • What was the question? How does D-ribose supplementation impact delayed onset muscle soreness (DOMS)? • How was it answered? Researchers conducted a single- blind randomized controlled trial. • Who was studied? Untrained healthy men participated in the study. • What was the intervention? Participants took 15 grams of D-ribose or placebo one hour before, and several times after, a series of plyometric frog jumps. • What's the main takeaway? Supplementation reduced DOMS by almost 50%, as measured by a subjective visual analog scale, up to 48 hours after exercise. • Any caveats? While DOMS was reduced, D-ribose had no clear influence on performance. Also, the researchers weren’t blinded to the condition, and the sample was small and limited to healthy untrained men. 23

What was the question? Can D-ribose supplementation reduce delayed onset muscle soreness? Why was the question worth asking? Delayed onset muscle soreness[1] (DOMS) is muscle soreness caused by muscle tissue damage from exercise. It is characterized by muscle tenderness or soreness that develops over 24 hours after exercise, remains at its peak for 24–72 hours, and tapers off within seven days. This extended post-exercise recovery is one of the most common issues that hinders exercise training and performance. Possible mechanistic causes include excess mechanical damage[2] that leads to inflammation, oxidative stress[3] (from increased mitochondrial respiration[4] as well as the tissue damage), and leakage of skeletal muscle enzymes[5] and proteins through damaged cell membranes. D-ribose[6] is a naturally occurring monosaccharide that is a key component of DNA, RNA, and the main energy currency in the body: adenosine triphosphate (ATP). Supplemental D-ribose has been shown to enhance recovery[7] of high-energy phosphates (such as ATP), presumably because it bypasses the slower enzymatic steps for its production from glucose and because D-ribose is a limiting factor[8] for ATP resy

recovery[7] of high-energy phosphates (such as ATP), presumably because it bypasses the slower enzymatic steps for its production from glucose and because D-ribose is a limiting factor[8] for ATP resynthesis (Figure 1). Some studies have demonstrated improved[9] exercise performance and/or recovery and reduced oxidative stress[10] following D-ribose supplementation. The reduction in oxidative stress could mitigate some of the downstream biochemical effects that lead to DOMS. However, there have been conflicting results[11] reported. It could be that training level[12] is a factor. That is, well-trained people tend to recover more quickly, on average, so D-ribose wouldn’t help them, which could explain some of the negative results. Figure 1: How supplemental D-ribose could replenish ATP faster 24

Reference: Mahoney et al. Adv Biosci Clin Med. 2018 May.[6] The authors of the study under review aimed to investigate whether D-ribose supplementation after a bout of eccentric, DOMS-inducing exercise in young untrained men could reduce DOMS symptoms and associated biomarkers. Evidence suggests that D-ribose can mitigate symptoms of delayed onset muscle soreness (DOMS) following exercise, but 25

some studies show conflicting results, which could depend on participant training level. The authors of the study set out to investigate the influence of D-ribose on DOMS in an untrained population. How was the question answered? The authors designed a single-blind (only participants did not know the treatment allocation) randomized controlled trial with 21 healthy untrained male participants to evaluate the impact of D- ribose supplementation on DOMS progression following a bout of plyometric exercise, in comparison to a placebo. They selected untrained people since they’d likely be more susceptible to DOMS, leaving more room for improvement through supplementation. The trial was not preregistered and primary or secondary outcomes were not distinguished. Once enrolled, participants completed a plyometric exercise (an explosive movement that involves rapid deceleration, transition, and acceleration in the opposite direction) routine involving 7 sets of 20 consecutive frog hops (squat down, jump forward) with 90 seconds of rest between each set. Participants were then matched according to BMI and muscle soreness and randomly assigned to take either D-ribose (n=11, 15 grams mixed in

own, jump forward) with 90 seconds of rest between each set. Participants were then matched according to BMI and muscle soreness and randomly assigned to take either D-ribose (n=11, 15 grams mixed in 200 milliliters of drinking water) or the placebo (n=10, same dose and taste of a calorically equivalent sweetener as the treatment) after a second round of plyometric exercises that was completed two weeks after the first. Supplements were administered one hour before and 1, 6, 12, and 36 hours post-exercise. Muscle soreness was assessed using a visual analog scale (0 = none, 10 = extreme) that participants were familiarized with before beginning the trial. Muscle strength was measured using a dynamometer that participants were also familiarized with before the trial. Blood samples were analyzed for creatine kinase, lactate dehydrogenase, and myoglobin as markers of muscle damage, and superoxide dismutase, total antioxidant capacity, and malondialdehyde as markers of oxidative stress. All measures were conducted at one hour before and 24 and 48 hours post- exercise. Researchers conducted a single-blind randomized controlled trial, involving 21 healthy untrained men who supplemented wi

measures were conducted at one hour before and 24 and 48 hours post- exercise. Researchers conducted a single-blind randomized controlled trial, involving 21 healthy untrained men who supplemented with D- 26

ribose or an isocaloric placebo following a bout of plyometric exercise. Muscle soreness and strength, as well as markers of muscle damage and oxidative stress, were monitored up to 48 hours after exercise to measure the impact of the intervention on the progression of DOMS. What was the answer? D-ribose supplementation decreased muscle soreness by 49% at 24 and 48 hours after the exercise, compared to placebo. All included measures of muscle damage, and one measure of muscle oxidative stress (malondialdehyde) were also reduced. Strength data suggested an attenuation of DOMS-associated reductions in muscle strength following D-ribose intake, but results were not statistically significant between groups. All participants completed both plyometric exercise sessions and reported an average muscle soreness of 5–6. No differences were found between groups in any measures at baseline. After a bout of plyometric exercise, D-ribose supplementation reduced muscle soreness, damage, and oxidative stress in healthy untrained men. However, it didn’t affect performance. How much should you trust the answer? It appears that the plyometric exercise was more than enough to induce DOMS. Previous stu

stress in healthy untrained men. However, it didn’t affect performance. How much should you trust the answer? It appears that the plyometric exercise was more than enough to induce DOMS. Previous studies that investigated the impact of plyometric exercise on performance, inflammation[13], soreness[14], and other health markers triggered a lower soreness VAS rating than the study under review. This is likely due to greater rest intervals and less exercise frequency. D-ribose supplementation, based on its physiological function in the human body, should theoretically improve recovery. The body can either[15] produce D-ribose from glucose, which is slow and depends on an enzyme that is often in short supply, or recycle ribose, assuming there is some to be recycled. Supplementation of D-ribose is a shortcut for both pathways, reducing the 27

need for the slow production process and providing a bulk supply to be used immediately. The presumed mechanism is that, with more ribose, the body can produce more nucleotides and ATP in response to damage and energy usage, respectively, that translates into faster recovery. However, this hasn’t been explicitly tested in the context of DOMS, so this mechanism is speculative. Overall, the underlying mechanisms are far from being well established. Given a presumed faster recovery that would allow for fewer muscle damage markers, lower inflammation, and increased ATP production, there may be some reason to believe that D-ribose can reduce oxidative stress. However, only one previous study (beyond the one under review) investigating oxidative stress[10] induced by a bout of exercise had results that suggest D-ribose can help improve these changes. This previous study also had a very different treatment protocol. The concept still needs more research to flesh out a concrete answer, especially since mitochondria (the cell’s ATP factory) create oxidative stress[16] when producing ATP, either aerobically or anaerobically[17], and if there’s more ATP production because D-ribose is suppleme

lly since mitochondria (the cell’s ATP factory) create oxidative stress[16] when producing ATP, either aerobically or anaerobically[17], and if there’s more ATP production because D-ribose is supplemented, more oxidative stress may be produced as well. The decreases in markers of muscle damage and soreness observed in the study under review also seem like they would make sense with the physiological understanding of D-ribose, but there is a lack of data to back up these findings in this specific context. The study[12] that only found an effect from D-ribose in untrained individuals reported improved ratings of perceived exertion and one marker of muscle health, creatine kinase. This supports the results of the study under review, but further studies evaluating D-ribose supplementation and its relationship with soreness, muscle damage, and oxidative stress are needed. Moreover, several markers[18] exist for muscle damage and researchers are not exactly clear on the relationships between all these markers and exercise- induced muscle damage, so a standardized method needs to be established. Finally, studies on trained individuals need to be conducted to be able to conclude whether or

all these markers and exercise- induced muscle damage, so a standardized method needs to be established. Finally, studies on trained individuals need to be conducted to be able to conclude whether or not trained individuals would benefit from supplementation, and through which underlying mechanism. Previous studies demonstrate conflicting results[19][20] regarding changes in strength reductions from exercise following D-ribose supplementation. While performance could be regarded as the most important metric when evaluating a sports supplement, D-ribose has not demonstrated [21]much promise for anaerobic activity[20] performance, as shown in the examples in Figure 2. Many differences in outcomes across the literature may have to do with varying dosage, type of exercise, duration of treatment, and other factors. A systematic review and meta-analysis would be very helpful in order to shed some light on these details, once more studies are conducted. Figure 2: Some instances in which D-ribose has not shown performance benefit 28
bose has not shown performance benefit 28

References: Dunne et al. Clin J Sport Med. 2006 Jan.[22]|Kerksick et al. Int J Sport Nutr Exerc Metab. 2005 Dec.[21]|Kreider et al. Int J Sport Nutr Exerc Metab. 2003 Mar. [20]|Berardi et al. J Strength Cond Res. 2003 Feb.[19] There are no adverse effects reported from short-term D-ribose supplementation, but reports on long term use are lacking[23]. 29

The study under review was single-blinded, meaning that only the participants did not know which treatment they were receiving, while the researchers knew the group each participant was assigned to. Whether consciously or unconsciously, researchers who know which participant receives which treatment might carry a bias[24] throughout data collection and analysis that ends up favoring a certain result. Since this was a pilot study (the first of its kind) with a small sample size, and there was little monitoring of compliance, and no dietary control, the generalizability of the reported results are limited. While the positive results from the study under review regarding D- ribose supplementation for DOMS symptoms and associated markers in untrained men may make physiological sense, they are quite preliminary, so further studies are needed to confirm the findings and broaden their applicability. What’s the take-home? After a bout of plyometric exercise by healthy untrained men, D-ribose supplementation was found to reduce muscle soreness, damage, and oxidative stress, but not the exercise-induced strength decline. This was a pilot study, so more controlled and careful research must be

upplementation was found to reduce muscle soreness, damage, and oxidative stress, but not the exercise-induced strength decline. This was a pilot study, so more controlled and careful research must be conducted to construct a data-driven understanding of the relationship between D-ribose and exercise recovery, rather than the current physiological speculation. Are you going to start taking D-ribose in an attempt to shorten your exercise recovery? Discuss in our NERD Facebook Forum! ^ Go back to table of contents 30

Interview: Jeff Rothschild, RD, CSSD, PhD(c) Tags: NERD Interview Jeff Rothschild is a Registered Dietitian and Board-Certified Specialist in Sports Dietetics. Jeff is currently doing his PhD research at Auckland University of Technology in the Sports Performance Research Institute New Zealand (SPRINZ) research lab, under the guidance of Dr. Dan Plews and Prof. Andrew Kilding. He is looking at the effects of pre-exercise nutrition on adaptations to endurance training, trying to better understand how what we eat before exercise affects the responses to exercise training. Q. You recently co-authored a review article exploring what we should eat before exercise. There, you described how carbohydrate restriction between training sessions can be used to lower muscle glycogen and thus increase fat oxidation. From a practical 33

standpoint, if someone is interested in reducing fat, could it be as simple as doing cardio after resistance training? Or would longer intervals between the first and second training session be required? Well, doing cardio after resistance training opens up the door to interference effects and the area of concurrent exercise training. But if someone’s goal is simply to stay fit and healthy, then yes, doing cardio at the end of a resistance training workout would be a fine idea. It really depends on the person’s goals, but it may be better to separate the sessions. One option to perhaps gain some additional benefit could be to do a resistance training session in the morning (i.e. before going to work), only consume protein afterwards, and then do a cardio session around midday before eating lunch. It’s also important to note that burning more fat (compared with carbohydrate) during exercise doesn’t necessarily mean it is the best way to reduce body fat. Q. The review paper also mentions that AMPK, which inhibits ATP-producing consuming pathways and stimulates ATP-producing pathways, can be activated to varying extents by exercise intensity, training status, muscle glycogen, and nutr

that AMPK, which inhibits ATP-producing consuming pathways and stimulates ATP-producing pathways, can be activated to varying extents by exercise intensity, training status, muscle glycogen, and nutrient availability. How quickly do we see this AMPK activation in response to training? Is this the type of thing someone needs to be concerned about within a particular training session? This happens quickly (i.e., during exercise), but it is not something people need to think much about since the only way to measure it is with a muscle biopsy. With that said, it is safe to assume that undertaking exercise with reduced glycogen levels (by restricting carbs in between two workouts within a short duration) may be able to up-regulate this signaling inside our muscles. Q. The review also mentions that, in general, being fasted or fed has a larger effect on performance than the size or timing of meals, and that fed exercise seems 34

to be better for performance. Is there any evidence that individuals who are accustomed to training in a fasted state have less of a dip in performance? The key thing here is likely to be the exercise duration. During shorter-duration exercise (less than 60 minutes), a placebo/belief effect is more likely to occur, and it could be possible for people to ‘convince’ themselves that their performance will suffer. For longer duration exercise, glycogen depletion becomes more of the issue and so I don’t think being accustomed to training fasted will help very much, presuming we’re talking about high-intensity exercise performance. There may be some mental benefit for people who are more used to the feeling of being a bit hungry, but I’m not sure how much that would affect things. I’m not sure there is a study testing this exactly, but in my research there hasn’t been any influence of habitual fasted training practices on performance in the fed vs. fasted state. Q. Similarly, you note that trained athletes tend to perform better after consuming carbohydrates prior to training, and that most athletes tend to believe their performance is better when they’ve eaten beforehand. Has there been

rained athletes tend to perform better after consuming carbohydrates prior to training, and that most athletes tend to believe their performance is better when they’ve eaten beforehand. Has there been any research that’s tested performance in people who don’t believe it matters whether they eat or not? In our survey of nearly 2,000 endurance athletes, about 50% disagreed with the statement “The quality of my workout is the same whether I eat or do not eat beforehand,” while the other half were split between ‘agree’ and ‘neutral’. So we might expect to see differences based on personal beliefs. But as noted above, even among people that didn’t expect to perform well in the fasted state, we’ve observed similar performance in the fed or fasted state when the exercise duration was around one hour. Q. What might mediate the improvement in time-to- fatigue seen in fasted athletes? That was seen in one study that found better improvements in time-to-fatigue at 85% VO2max in 35

trained cyclists performing four weeks of sprint interval training in the fasted state compared to those that consumed carbohydrate before and during exercise. In this case, I suspect it might be more to do with carbohydrate blunting the training adaptations, as opposed to fasted training improving it to an additional degree. To clarify, there seems to be the potential for carbohydrates to negatively impact training adaptations when consumed in very high amounts (and I think the amount is the key thing). In that study, the sprint training consisted of four to seven 30-second sprints, performed three times per week for four weeks. So it was a very low volume of exercise. The carbohydrate group consumed a very large amount of carbs both before and during exercise (2.5 grams per kilogram of bodyweight before, and sipping on sports drink during, training sessions). So it’s possible that the huge amount of carbs consumed before exercise (about 200 grams) could have blunted some intracellular signaling in response to the training and blunted some of the adaptations. However, I wouldn’t expect to see that result if the carb group had consumed something more modest like 50–75 grams before

r signaling in response to the training and blunted some of the adaptations. However, I wouldn’t expect to see that result if the carb group had consumed something more modest like 50–75 grams before exercise. There’s also the confounding factor of consuming the carb drink during exercise, which can influence things on its own. Q. Should carbohydrate periodization be done on a micro, meso, or macro scale? In other words, should athletes spend days, weeks, or months in high or low- carbohydrate training conditions? The prevailing sense is that it should vary on a micro scale—that is, day-to-day or even meal-to- meal. You can think of the last workout you had, and the next workout you have coming up, and load the fuel tank appropriately. With that said, it would also likely vary through the year as the training intensity and volume would also be changing through the year. So if you’re eating to match your training on a day-to-day basis and most of your training is lower intensity and/or volume for a period of weeks or months, then your food should match closer to that. Q. To wrap things up, can you run us through the practical takeaways of your research by answering the question the

volume for a period of weeks or months, then your food should match closer to that. Q. To wrap things up, can you run us through the practical takeaways of your research by answering the question the paper poses: What should we eat before exercise? On one hand, for most people it probably doesn’t matter that much. I feel odd saying that, but the training itself is going to matter so much more. But going beyond that, the duration and intensity of 36

the exercise session should be considered when deciding on the best pre-exercise nutrition choices. Before shorter duration exercise sessions that focus on lower intensity steady-state training, it may be beneficial to withhold carbohydrate, but there is little reason to think there are benefits to avoiding carbohydrate before high-intensity exercise. So assuming you’re consuming less than approximately 75 grams of carbohydrate, food choices before high-intensity training sessions can be left to personal preference. For longer duration exercise (more than 90 minutes), there is little evidence to suggest fasted-state training offers any additional benefit, although this is still practiced by many endurance athletes. Ingesting less than approximately 75 grams of carbohydrate is unlikely to impair mitochondrial signaling adaptations from longer-duration, low- intensity exercise, while consuming 75–150 grams of carbohydrate prior to extended high-intensity exercise is suggested to increase endogenous fuel storage. Overall, I think there is more leeway than people often think. As mentioned in the above study, consuming 200 grams of carbohydrate before a short interval session is probabl

endogenous fuel storage. Overall, I think there is more leeway than people often think. As mentioned in the above study, consuming 200 grams of carbohydrate before a short interval session is probably going too far and might be disadvantageous. But if you’re consuming around 75 grams or less, I don’t see much reason to think it will have undesirable effects. Figure 1: What should we eat before we exercise? It depends!
