Deep Dive: Will nitrates improve your training performance? It depends! Tags: Nitrate, Supplementation, Exercise Performance, Aerobic Study under review: Ergogenic Effect of Nitrate Supplementation: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis Quick Takes • What was the question? How does nitrate supplementation impact performance? • How was it answered? Researchers completed a meta- analysis of randomized controlled trials. • Who was studied? Participants were healthy people, mostly 18–40 years old. Men were much more highly represented than women. • What was the intervention? Participants supplemented nitrate 40–140 minutes before exercise, with doses ranging from roughly 1–30 mmol (about 60–1,800 milligrams). The studies used both acute dosing (one dose before exercise) and chronic dosing (multiple doses over several days). Outcomes included a wide variety of mostly aerobic metrics such as cycling or running performance. • What's the main takeaway? Overall, nitrate 40

supplementation had a small but statistically significant effect on performance outcomes. The effects were strongest (but still very small to small) from doses of more than 5 mmol (310 milligrams) per day, after at least one day of supplementation, and when supplementation occured 2–3.5 hours before training. These effects were only observed in participants with lower baseline aerobic fitness. • Any caveats? Although the authors reported on publication bias, they didn’t report on the quality of the included studies, making it difficult to determine how accurate these estimates were. Also, there was no statistically significant effect found on performance measures for knee extension and rowing. This could be due to a combination of small effect sizes and little data. Since these two exercises were very underrepresented, it’s quite possible that more data could reveal a significant (but probably small) effect. Introduction Both athletes and recreationally active people commonly use dietary supplements in an attempt to improve their physical performance. One such supplement is nitrate (NO3−), a molecule which is produced in the body in small amounts, and which is obtainable through th

ry supplements in an attempt to improve their physical performance. One such supplement is nitrate (NO3−), a molecule which is produced in the body in small amounts, and which is obtainable through the diet mainly through the consumption of some vegetables[1] (e.g., lettuce, beetroot, and spinach). In the body, dietary nitrate is reduced to nitrite, and then to nitric oxide (NO) through what is called the nitrate-nitrite-nitric oxide pathway[2], shown in Figure 1. NO, through its effects on vasodilation [3], mitochondrial respiration[4], calcium handling[5], and glucose uptake[6], may improve muscle function and reduce fatigue, thereby enhancing physical performance. 41
Figure 1: How dietary nitrate may ultimately boost performance 42

Reference: Jones. Sports Med. 2014 May.[7] While NO3−supplementation should, theoretically, improve training performance, there is substantial variability in its ergogenic effects within and between trials. This variability could be due to several factors, including biological sex, aerobic fitness level, environmental oxygen concentrations, exercise parameters, and the dosage and timing of NO3−supplementation. The meta-analysis under review pooled the available trials together to examine the overall impact of nitrate supplementation on training performance, and is the first meta-analysis to have examined the potential influence of the aforementioned factors on NO3−ergogenicity. Although nitrate supplementation is common in athletes and recreationally active people looking to improve their physical performance, there is substantial variability in the ergogenic effects of nitrate within and between trials. This variability could be due to several individual, environmental, exercise, and supplementation- related factors. The meta-analysis under review aimed to examine the potential influence of these factors. What was studied? This was a systematic review and meta-analysis that was de

supplementation- related factors. The meta-analysis under review aimed to examine the potential influence of these factors. What was studied? This was a systematic review and meta-analysis that was designed to evaluate the efficacy of NO3− supplementation for improving training performance, and examined the potential influence of biological sex, aerobic fitness level, environmental oxygen concentrations, exercise parameters, and the dosage and timing of NO3−supplementation on NO3−efficacy. Eligible studies had to be randomized, single or double-blind, crossover, and placebo-controlled, and carried out in healthy people with a mean study age of 18–40 years. Ultimately, 80 studies were included. Of these, two presented data from men and women separately, six presented data from different NO3−supplementation protocols separately, seven presented data from different exercises separately, and eight presented data from different environmental oxygen concentrations separately, resulting in 113 data sets. The studies involved a total of 1,135 participants (1,179 men and 156 women) with each study including 6–36 participants. The majority of studies (n=52) measured performance through time

3 data sets. The studies involved a total of 1,135 participants (1,179 men and 156 women) with each study including 6–36 participants. The majority of studies (n=52) measured performance through time trials, with almost all of the others using other measures related to aerobic endurance. Only 13 trials looked at strength or power, and those examined effects on reduction of strength or power through fatigue. 44

To assess individual study quality, the meta-analysis used the Cochrane Collaboration’s tool for assessing risk of bias in RCTs. Bias was assessed as high, low, or unclear over seven components: (i) sequence generation, (ii) allocation concealment, (iii) blinding of participants and personnel, (iv) blinding of outcome assessors, (v) incomplete outcome data, (vi) selective outcome reporting, and (vii) other sources of bias. However, the risk of bias assessment was not fully presented in the paper. Publication bias was assessed using a funnel plot, and no publication bias was detected. The studies were pooled in a random effects model, and the standardized mean difference (SMD) was used to determine the size of the effect, where an SMD of 0.15–0.2 was defined as very small, 0.2–0.5 as small, 0.5–0.8 as moderate, and more than 0.8 as large. The study was not preregistered. This was a systematic review and meta-analysis evaluating the efficacy of NO3−supplementation for improving training performance, and examining the potential influence of several individual, environmental, exercise-related, and supplementation- related factors on NO3−efficacy. The meta-analysis included 80 crossover

performance, and examining the potential influence of several individual, environmental, exercise-related, and supplementation- related factors on NO3−efficacy. The meta-analysis included 80 crossover and placebo-controlled trials carried out in 1,135 healthy young adults (1,179 men and 156 women). What were the findings? In the overall analysis, NO3−supplementation improved performance, compared to placebo, with a very small effect size (SMD=0.174; CI: 0.12–0.23). In the subgroup analyses by biological sex, NO3−supplementation outperformed placebo in men- only and mixed sex analyses, but the effect was smaller and not statistically significant the women- only analysis. In the subgroup analyses by aerobic fitness level, NO3−supplementation outperformed placebo in the first four aerobic fitness categories (VO2peak of less than 50, 50–54.9, 55–59.9, and 60–64.9 mL/kg/min) with an effect size hovering around very small to small (SMD of less than 0.25), but not in the highest aerobic fitness category (VO2peak of more than 65 mL/kg/ min). In the subgroup analyses by environmental oxygen concentrations, NO3−supplementation 45
ss category (VO2peak of more than 65 mL/kg/ min). In the subgroup analyses by environmental oxygen concentrations, NO3−supplementation 45

outperformed placebo under both normoxia and hypoxia. In the subgroup analyses by exercise duration, NO3−supplementation outperformed placebo in all exercise duration categories (less than 300, 301–600, 601–999, and 1,000+ seconds), with the effect size being smaller in the longest- duration exercise category (1,000+ seconds or 17+ minutes). In the subgroup analyses by exercise type, NO3−supplementation outperformed placebo in cycling, running, and handgrip exercises, but not in knee extension and rowing. In the subgroup analyses by NO3−dosage, supplementation with more than 5 mmol per day (310 milligrams per day) outperformed placebo, while the effect did not reach statistical significance with doses under 5 mmol per day. In the subgroup analyses by supplementation duration in days, NO3− supplementation outperformed placebo in all categories (i.e., 1, 2–6, and more than 7 days). Finally, in the subgroup analyses by supplementation timing, NO3−supplementation outperformed placebo when the supplement was taken 2–3.5 hours before training, but not when taken closer to training (i.e., less than 2 hours). The subgroup analyses are a handful, so their main takeaways are summarized in Fi

the supplement was taken 2–3.5 hours before training, but not when taken closer to training (i.e., less than 2 hours). The subgroup analyses are a handful, so their main takeaways are summarized in Figure 2. Figure 2: Main takeaways from the subgroup analyses 46

NO3−supplementation slightly improved performance, as compared to placebo. In the subgroup analyses, the effects were significant in men-only and mixed sex trials, in participants with VO2peak of less than 64.9 mL/kg/min, in both normoxia and hypoxia, with all exercise durations, and with cycling, running, and handgrip exercises. The effects were also significant with NO3− dosages more than 5 mmol per day (310 milligrams per day), with at least one day of supplementation, and when NO3−was taken 2–3.5 hours before training. 47

The bigger picture The study under review found a very small (about 3%) but statistically significant effect of NO3− supplementation for improving training performance. These results seem to support the mechanistic evidence relating to nitrate’s potential ergogenic effects, and are in agreement with previous narrative[7] reviews[8] on the topic. It did, however, also confirm that there are substantial differences in the efficacy of NO3−ergogenicity between trials, with the subsequent subgroup analyses suggesting that the ergogenic effects of NO3−are more apparent in sedentary or recreationally trained adults (as opposed to highly trained athletes), and with higher-intensity, shorter-duration exercise (i.e., less than 16 minutes). In the subgroup analyses according to sex, NO3−supplementation improved performance in men- only and mixed sex trials, but not in women-only trials. While the reasons for the differential ergogenic effects of NO3−in men and women are not clear, they may relate to anthropometric, metabolic, and physiological differences[9] between the sexes that may affect the biological effects of NO3−in the body. Considering that the effect size of NO3−in women wasn’t tha

to anthropometric, metabolic, and physiological differences[9] between the sexes that may affect the biological effects of NO3−in the body. Considering that the effect size of NO3−in women wasn’t that much smaller, it may be that it didn’t reach statistical significance just due to the low number of women-only trials included in the meta-analysis (n=6), which may have rendered the analysis underpowered to detect an effect. Moreover, NO3−supplementation failed to improve performance in people with the highest aerobic fitness level (i.e., VO2peak of more than 65 mL/kg/min). This finding is in agreement with a 2013 meta-analysis[10], which found that NO3−supplementation improved training performance in non- athletes (VO2peak of less than 65 mL/kg/min) but not in athletes (VO2peak of more than 65 mL/kg/ min). One potential explanation for the blunted ergogenic effects of NO3−supplementation in athletes is that nitric oxide synthase activity is increased[11] in highly trained individuals, which may mean that the nitrate-nitrite-nitric oxide pathway becomes less important for NO production. On the other hand, some research challenges this explanation, and, instead, suggests that highly

which may mean that the nitrate-nitrite-nitric oxide pathway becomes less important for NO production. On the other hand, some research challenges this explanation, and, instead, suggests that highly trained individuals may have higher[12] levels of plasma nitrite (due to eating a superior diet), which could result in a smaller response to NO3−supplementation. In the subgroup analyses examining the influence of exercise duration, NO3−supplementation improved training performance in all exercise duration categories. However, the effect size was smaller when exercise lasted approximately more than 16 minutes. This makes sense from a physiological standpoint, as longer-duration, lower-intensity training results in a reduced[7] requirement for production of NO through the nitrate-nitrite-nitric oxide pathway. With regard to exercise type, NO3−supplementation led to small improvements in cycling and running performance, and non-significant improvements in knee extension and rowing performance. While, at first glance, these results would suggest that the ergogenic effects of NO3− 48
ce. While, at first glance, these results would suggest that the ergogenic effects of NO3− 48

supplementation may depend on exercise type, it should be kept in mind that the majority of the trials looked at cycling (n=61) and running (n=28) performance, with only a limited number of studies examining knee extension (n=10) and rowing (n=5) performance. Considering that the effect sizes were similar for these exercise types, it’s likely that the analyses for knee extension and rowing were simply underpowered to detect a significant effect. On the other hand, NO3− supplementation led to much larger improvements in handgrip exercise performance. This suggests that the ergogenic effects of NO3−may be greater in small muscle groups exercises, where performance is likely limited by peripheral factors rather than cardiac output. However, note that the handgrip subgroup analysis included only four trials. The results from the subgroup analyses according to NO3−supplementation parameters suggest that daily doses of more than 5 mmol (310 milligrams) of NO3−are required to elicit a potent ergogenic effect, that even one day of supplementation is enough to improve training performance, and that NO3−should be taken 2–3.5 hours before training. These results are in line with the recommend

ergogenic effect, that even one day of supplementation is enough to improve training performance, and that NO3−should be taken 2–3.5 hours before training. These results are in line with the recommendations of previous research[13]. One strength of this meta-analysis is the use of a random effects model to analyze the data, as this shows that the researchers didn’t assume that the trials employed similar methodologies. The random effects model also tends to result in wider confidence intervals than a fixed effects model, and is more conservative in detecting significant results, which means that the researchers can be more confident that the detected effects are real. This meta-analysis has some noteworthy limitations. First, although a risk of bias assessment was performed, it was not fully presented. As such, it’s unclear if the results of the meta-analysis are based on high quality evidence. Second, heterogeneity was not assessed (or at least reported) for any of the analyses. Third, some of the subgroups (e.g., the women-only subgroup, and the handgrip, knee extension, and rowing subgroups) included a small number of trials, which means that they may have been underpowered to d

, some of the subgroups (e.g., the women-only subgroup, and the handgrip, knee extension, and rowing subgroups) included a small number of trials, which means that they may have been underpowered to detect significant effects. Finally, it’s important to consider the generalizability of the results. The studies included in the meta-analysis included young, healthy, adult participants, which means that the results can’t be generalized to older populations or to people with health conditions. NO3−supplementation has a significant, but very small, benefit for training performance under both normoxia and hypoxia in young, healthy adults who are sedentary or recreationally trained, but not in highly trained athletes. These ergogenic effects may become 49

smaller with longer exercise durations. While the results suggest that the ergogenicity of NO3−supplementation may be blunted in women, and in knee extension and rowing exercises, more research is needed before definitive conclusions can be drawn. Frequently asked questions Q. How do I convert nitrate from mmol to milligrams? One mmol corresponds to 62 milligrams of NO3−. This means that the ergogenic daily NO3−dose of 5–25 mmol per day corresponds to 310–1,550 milligrams, or approximately 200–1,000 grams of beetroot. Q. Can I get an ergogenic dose of nitrates from my diet alone? According to one study[14], “normal” omnivorous diets supply approximately 74 milligrams of NO3− per day, while vegetarian diets supply around 267 milligrams per day. So, in short, yes, although you would have to increase your intake of specific vegetables that are especially high in NO3−. The rough nitrate contents of various foods are shown in Figure 3. Figure 3: The approximate nitrate content of different foods 50
foods 50

Reference: Hord et al. Am J Clin Nutr. 2009 Jul.[15] Q. Are isolated nitrate supplements more or less effective than beet/vegetable extracts high in nitrate? Almost 90% of the available trials looking at the ergogenicity of nitrates have used vegetable extracts (almost exclusively beetroot juice) as a form of NO3−supplementation. Out of the remaining 10% of the trials that have used isolated nitrate supplements (specifically, sodium or potassium nitrate) there seems to be[10] roughly an equal split between trials finding a significant or non-significant ergogenic effect. This is largely in line with the results of the overall research on nitrate ergogenicity, and suggests that isolated nitrate supplements are similarly effective to vegetable extracts. However, more trials using isolated nitrate supplements are needed before any conclusions can be drawn with certainty. What should I know? There is substantial variability in the ergogenic effects of NO3−supplementation within and between trials, which is likely due to several individual, environmental, exercise-related, and supplementation-related factors. This meta-analysis examined the influence of these factors. According to the r

trials, which is likely due to several individual, environmental, exercise-related, and supplementation-related factors. This meta-analysis examined the influence of these factors. According to the results of this meta-analysis, NO3−supplementation in young, healthy adults has a very small effect on improving training performance that is unaffected by environmental oxygen levels. However, the improvements are not experienced by highly trained athletes, and the effects may be smaller with longer exercise durations. While the ergogenicity of NO3−supplementation may be blunted in women due to biological sex differences, and in knee extension and rowing exercises, only a handful of trials were included in these subgroups, so more research is needed to draw definitive conclusions. Nitrate’s ergogenic effects were optimized when participants took a daily dose of about 5–25mmol (310–1,550 milligrams) 2–3.5 hours before exercise after at least one day of prior supplementation. Can sweet and salty mouth rinses provide a taste of victory? Tags: Mouth Rinse, Exercise Performance, Endurance Training Study under review: Effect of glucose and sodium chloride mouth rinses on neuromuscular fatigue: a preliminary study Quick Takes • What question this study addressed: Can using sugar or salt mouth rinses while cycling attenuate reduction in muscle function? • Who this study applies to: Young, healthy men. • What the intervention was: Using a 6% sodium chloride or 6% glucose mouth rinse immediately before, and every 10 minutes during, a 30-minute cycling bout at 70% of maximal oxygen uptake. • What the main takeaway is: Compared to a water rinse, both the salt and sugar mouth rinses mitigated the decline in muscle contraction after the cycling bout. • Caveats: The study could not be blinded, was not preregistered, and had a very small sample size. Also, the primary outcome of muscular contraction is not a direct predictor of real-world sports performance. More studies are 55
es are 55

needed in a wider variety of conditions to confirm this finding and to determine if it indicates actual benefits for performance. What was the question? The researchers designed the study to determine whether salt and carbohydrate mouth rinses could improve the perseverance of maximal voluntary contraction following a 30-minute cycling exercise at 70% of the participants’ maximal oxygen uptake. Why was the question worth asking? A 2004 hallmark study[1] found that using a carbohydrate mouth rinse solution could augment performance by stimulating receptors in the oral cavity. Since then, multiple studies have tried to pin down the exact mechanisms through which this effect could arise for both shorter[3] and longer [3] distance exercises. Upon tasting sweetness, there is a gustatory excitation[4] response in the body, during which receptors that reside in the oral cavity activate the parts of the brain[5] that drive exertion[6] during exercise. Scientists have proposed that the taste of sweetness exciting these areas leads to a response from these areas that counters the decline in potentially performance relevant neuro- muscular activation. In other words, merely tasting sweet thin

sweetness exciting these areas leads to a response from these areas that counters the decline in potentially performance relevant neuro- muscular activation. In other words, merely tasting sweet things could excite parts of the nervous system, which could have ergogenic benefits. But carbohydrates aren’t the only tastes that can stimulate regions of the brain that are important for movement—other tastes could do this, too[7]. There are a lot of taste buds[8] throughout the oral cavity, as well as in the gastrointestinal tract. Some respond to sweet sensations, others respond to salty or bitter ones. It’s quite possible that stimulating these other taste receptors could provide a similar ergogenic effect as carbohydrate mouth rinses, through a similar mechanism. In fact, there’s some evidence of this effect. For example, salt has been found to acutely produce an increase in muscle contraction force[9] and can cause a neurobiological change in brain regions[10] associated with endurance exercise performance. Since it is common practice for athletes to consume both carbohydrates and electrolytes[11] (read: salts) during exercise, the authors of the study under review had a plausible

exercise performance. Since it is common practice for athletes to consume both carbohydrates and electrolytes[11] (read: salts) during exercise, the authors of the study under review had a plausible reason to investigate 56

whether swirling a salt solution around the mouth and then spitting it out could have a positive (or detrimental) effect on athletic performance. Carbohydrate mouth rinses stimulate areas of the brain that drive exertion during exercise, which could lead to ergogenic benefits. Other tastes, like salt, could have similar effects, in theory. The authors of the study under review aimed to test this hypothesis. How was the question answered? Ten active and young (average age of 22) males free of any metabolic diseases and/or injuries that would prevent them from completing the trials were recruited for the study. Participants were moderately fit, and had an above-average[12] maximal oxygen uptake of 48.3 ml/kg/min. Upon successful recruitment, the research team implemented a randomized cross-over design with a seven-day washout period between the three conditions: (1) water, (2) 6% glucose, or (3) 6% salt mouth rinse solutions. Participants were asked to refrain from exercise, caffeine, and alcohol consumption 24 hours prior to the trial. Participants arrived at the lab for testing in a fasted state. They warmed up and then performed three sets of maximal isometric knee extensions, wit

d alcohol consumption 24 hours prior to the trial. Participants arrived at the lab for testing in a fasted state. They warmed up and then performed three sets of maximal isometric knee extensions, with a two-minute recovery between sets. The highest power produced from the three sets was used for the maximal voluntary contraction (MVC). An additional maximal isometric knee extension was performed for 90 seconds to assess sustained MVC (sMVC). Once baseline measurements were taken, participants cycled for 30 minutes at 70% of their maximal oxygen uptake while rinsing their mouth out (five-second rinse) with one of the trial solutions in undisclosed amounts immediately before and every 10 minutes during the trial. The participants partook in MVC and sMVC testing once more before exiting the laboratory. The study was not preregistered, no power calculations were performed, and no primary outcomes were declared. Moreover, there was no blinding of the treatments due to the fundamentally different tastes of the treatments. Changes in ten active young men’s maximal and sustained knee 57
ferent tastes of the treatments. Changes in ten active young men’s maximal and sustained knee 57

isometric contraction were measured following 30 minutes of cycling after swishing with either water, glucose solution, or salt solution every 10 minutes throughout the cycling exercise. What was the answer? Post-cycling testing revealed a significant reduction in MVC and sMVC for all conditions. However, glucose and salt mouth rinsing at 6% solutions preserved MVC and sMVC to a greater extent when compared to water, with no significant differences between glucose or salt mouth rinsing trials. Figure 1 shows that MVC dropped roughly 15% in the glucose and salt conditions, compared to a 35% drop when swishing water. 58

Figure 1: Percent drop in MVC in the three conditions How much should you trust the answer? The results of the study suggest that, in active and fasted men, a 6% carbohydrate or salt solution provided before and every 10 minutes during exercise preserves the participants’ ability to produce force and sustained muscular contraction when compared to a water beverage. A large portion of the research supports these findings[13], as well as the idea that fasting results in taste receptors in the mouth and gut being more sensitive, thus sending a greater putatively ergogenic stimulus to the brain regions. Carbohydrate mouth rinsing, in particular, has been shown to improve performance in untrained[14], trained[15], endurance[3], and strength training[16] scenarios. However, there is also a great deal of literature suggesting carbohydrate mouth rinsing 59

doesn’t work. Many of the studies that provided these results were done either in a fed state[17], in the heat[18], during repeated sprints[19], during time trials in trained cyclists[17] or included participants who were and remained euhydrated[20]. It shouldn’t go unmentioned that shoddy statistical analysis that has plagued the world of sport science[21] could be to blame for overestimating positive findings in highly trained athletes partaking in MVC efforts[16] in a fatigued state or during prolonged submaximal cycling exercise[22]. Or if ingesting a carbohydrate beverage[23] would elicit a better performance-enhancing effect than swishing and spitting. In view of the heterogeneous study results of sweet mouth rinses, it is all the more warranted to narrow the focus of the discussion to the effects of rinsing with essentially salt water. As mentioned earlier, salt taste receptors are found throughout the oral cavity, influence insular cortex and/or opioid receptor activity[24], and in turn, directly or indirectly affect autonomic activity[25] and fatigue susceptibility[26]. From that, it’s reasonable to infer that rinsing your mouth with common electrolyte beverages acts as a

d in turn, directly or indirectly affect autonomic activity[25] and fatigue susceptibility[26]. From that, it’s reasonable to infer that rinsing your mouth with common electrolyte beverages acts as a way to minimize fatigue and preserve your ability to produce force over time. In fact, this latest addition to the ‘mouth rinsing for exercise performance’ research doesn’t only suggest that salt works, but also that it is as effective as glucose when it comes to the amelioration of exercise- induced reductions in muscular contractility. However, follow-up studies are greatly needed before any conclusions can be stated with confidence, especially given the relatively small sample size and highly specific study population of young fit men in the study at hand. In addition to sample size and population considerations, there are some other issues regarding the study design that may limit placing too much confidence in its findings. First, the participants couldn’t be blinded to the intervention because salt tastes different than sweetness, leaving open the possibility that participants could have been primed to perform differently based on their previous beliefs about tastes. Researchers

on because salt tastes different than sweetness, leaving open the possibility that participants could have been primed to perform differently based on their previous beliefs about tastes. Researchers have noted that placebos have neurobiological effects[27] and a recent review highlights the performance-enhancing effects of rinsing one’s mouth out with a carbohydrate solution could be, in part, explained by a placebo (or nocebo) effect[28]. Second, MVC isn’t a direct measure of what most athletes actually care about: performance. So, it’s open to question whether these results would have practical effects in terms of real-world performance benefits, such as improved time to exhaustion, better time-trial performance, etc. Third, the authors could have benefited from an additional, fourth arm to the study, in which they combined the carbohydrate and salt sources into one. This would have been useful because it is common practice for athletes to consume both carbohydrates and electrolytes[11] during exercise. Also, there’s some physiological reasons to suspect a possible synergy between salty and sweet 60
[11] during exercise. Also, there’s some physiological reasons to suspect a possible synergy between salty and sweet 60

tastes, such as the existence of a sodium-dependent glucose transporter[29] (SGLT1) present in taste receptors, which requires taking up sodium along with glucose. This study replicated similar effects found in studies involving carbohydrate mouth rinses while extending them to salt rinses also. However, these results should be considered preliminary, and ideally should be replicated in larger, more diverse participant groups and in studies that introduce a no-rinse condition or combined treatment with both carbs and salt. What’s the take-home? This study confirmed that rinsing with a 6% carbohydrate solution has ergogenic effects on one’s ability to produce force after a fatiguing bout of exercise in a fasted state. In addition, it found that similar benefits can also be had by rinsing with a 6% salt solution. More research in larger, more diverse populations that focus specifically on sports performance outcomes would be useful to confirm this effect and determine whether it has any practical benefits in a wider range of athletes. Not just for making cakes: baking soda may improve exercise performance Tags: Sodium Bicarbonate, Endurance Training, Muscle Protein Synthesis Study under review: Effects of Sodium Bicarbonate Supplementation on Muscular Strength and Endurance: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis Quick Takes • What question this study addressed: Does supplementing sodium bicarbonate before exercise acutely improve muscular strength or endurance? • How the question was answered: Through meta-analyses of randomized controlled trials. • Who was studied: Almost exclusively males, ranging in fitness levels from athletes to untrained individuals. • What the intervention was: Almost all trials used 0.3 grams of sodium bicarbonate per kilogram of bodyweight one to three hours before exercise. • What the main takeaway is: Supplementing before exercise led to small improvements in muscular endurance, but there was no clear effect on muscular strength. • Caveats: The total and individual number of participants was very low, leaving room for more research, especially in 64
s was very low, leaving room for more research, especially in 64

women. The evidence to date is consistent with effect sizes ranging from trivial to moderate, and may very well vary depending on training status and possibly muscle group, given the differences in participants and muscles tested between trials. What was the question? This meta-analysis was designed to determine whether supplementation with sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3) before exercise can improve muscular strength and endurance. Why was the question worth asking? Sodium bicarbonate, also known as baking soda or its chemical nomenclature NaHCO3, is a common household item used for a variety of purposes, including baking, pest control, fire extinguishing, cleaning, and personal hygiene. In 2018, it was included in the International Olympic Committee consensus statement[1] as a supplement with good evidence for improving exercise performance. Sodium bicarbonate is thought to improve exercise performance by increasing the concentration of blood bicarbonate, a buffer that can help maintain acid-base balance[2]. Specifically, muscle fatigue during exercise is thought to take place, in part, due to exercise-induced acidosis[3] (i.e. a decrease in pH). This means that the onset of muscle fa

cid-base balance[2]. Specifically, muscle fatigue during exercise is thought to take place, in part, due to exercise-induced acidosis[3] (i.e. a decrease in pH). This means that the onset of muscle fatigue is partly dependent on the body’s ability to buffer decreases in pH. Buffering agents like sodium bicarbonate[4] neutralize hydrogen ions (H+), helping to delay decreases in pH, thereby delaying muscle fatigue and improving exercise performance. This process is depicted in Figure 1. Figure 1: How bicarbonate buffers acidity during exercise 65

Reference: Todd. Biosci Horiz. 2014 Jun. DOI: 10.1093/biohorizons/hzu001 While the general ergogenic effects of sodium bicarbonate and its physiological modes of action are relatively well-established, its practical benefits, specifically on muscular strength and endurance, are not. This is because the available trials that have investigated sodium bicarbonate’s effects on muscular strength and endurance have produced conflicting results; and although several meta-analyses[5][6][7] have explored the effects of sodium bicarbonate on various aspects of exercise performance (with predominantly positive results for sustained high-intensity exercise with high anaerobic metabolic demands), none have focused specifically on muscular strength and endurance. The goal of the study under review was to meta-analyze the available trials in order to determine the overall impact of acute sodium bicarbonate supplementation on muscular strength and endurance. The well-established ergogenic effects of sodium bicarbonate coupled with mechanistic evidence on its physiological modes of action suggest that acute sodium bicarbonate supplementation 66
odium bicarbonate coupled with mechanistic evidence on its physiological modes of action suggest that acute sodium bicarbonate supplementation 66

may have positive effects on muscular strength and endurance. However, the available relevant trials have produced conflicting results, prompting the study authors to conduct this meta-analysis. How was the question answered? The authors tried to answer the question by conducting a systematic review and meta-analysis of crossover trials that looked at the effects of acute sodium bicarbonate supplementation on muscular strength and endurance in healthy individuals. In the trials included in the meta-analysis, muscular strength was assessed by changes in peak force, peak torque, or maximum load lifted, while muscular endurance was assessed by changes in the number of repetitions performed, isokinetic total work, or time to maintain isometric force production. Ultimately, 20 trials were included in the meta-analysis, with 13 trials looking at muscular endurance, and 11 looking at muscular strength. The total number of participants across all trials was 192 (median number of trial participants was 10), with 184 men and eight women. As shown in Figure 2, the participants of the trials included untrained, recreationally trained, and resistance- trained individuals, as well as athletes. O

ants was 10), with 184 men and eight women. As shown in Figure 2, the participants of the trials included untrained, recreationally trained, and resistance- trained individuals, as well as athletes. Out of the 20 trials included, 19 used a sodium bicarbonate dose of 0.3 grams per kilogram of bodyweight, and one used a dose of 0.4 grams per kilogram of bodyweight. The timing of ingestion ranged from 60 to 180 minutes before exercise. Figure 2: Major study characteristics 67

The muscular performance data of the included trials were converted to standardized mean differences (SMD) and summarized in a random-effects model. Subgroup analyses were performed for the muscular endurance of small vs. large muscle groups, and for muscular strength in a rested vs. fatigued state. Heterogeneity was tested using the I2 statistic, while the methodological quality of the included trials was assessed with the Physiotherapy Evidence Database[8] (PEDro) checklist. The study under review was conducted according to PRISMA guidelines, but does not appear to have been preregistered. This was a systematic review and meta-analysis of 20 trials involving a total of 192 healthy participants (of which 184 were men) that explored the effects of supplementation with 0.3-0.4 grams of sodium bicarbonate per kilogram of bodyweight 60 to 180 minutes before exercise on muscular strength and endurance. 68

What was the answer? The meta-analysis found that, in healthy individuals, acute sodium bicarbonate supplementation had a small positive effect on muscular endurance (SMD = 0.37; 95% CI: 0.15, 0.59) that decreased in size, but remained significant (SMD = 0.20; 95% CI: 0.05, 0.36), after researchers excluded one study[9] that reported a very large effect. In the subgroup analyses for this outcome, sodium bicarbonate improved muscular endurance for both large (SMD = 0.40; 95% CI: 0.13, 0.66) and small (SMD = 0.31; 95% CI: 0.04, 0.59) muscle groups. For muscular strength, there were no significant effects of sodium bicarbonate supplementation (SMD = -0.03; 95% CI: -0.18, 0.12) whether the testing was carried out in a rested state (SMD = 0.02; 95% CI: -0.09, 0.13) or in a fatigued state (SMD = −0.16; 95% CI: -0.59, 0.28). Of the trials included in the meta-analysis, 15 were rated as being of excellent methodological quality, four trials were assessed as good, and one trial was assessed as fair. None of the studies were assessed as being of poor methodological quality. Moderate levels of heterogeneity (I2 = 70-73%) were detected between trials for the primary outcomes. The findings of t

fair. None of the studies were assessed as being of poor methodological quality. Moderate levels of heterogeneity (I2 = 70-73%) were detected between trials for the primary outcomes. The findings of this meta-analysis indicate that sodium bicarbonate supplementation before exercise can improve muscular endurance in both large and small muscle groups. It’s unlikely to improve muscular strength in either a rested or fatigued state, however. How much should you trust the answer? The observed positive effects of sodium bicarbonate on muscular endurance make sense from a physiological standpoint. This is because muscular endurance tasks can result[10] in high production of H+, and sodium bicarbonate is thought to elicit its performance-enhancing effects by removing H+ from the working muscle, helping to delay decreases in pH, and, consequently, delaying muscle fatigue. That said, it should be noted that the size of the effect was small, especially after one study[9] with a very large effect size was removed (SMD = 0.20). Moreover, the 95% confidence intervals, which range from 0.15 (a trivial effect) to 0.59 (a medium effect size), allow for a wide range of effect sizes individual train

size was removed (SMD = 0.20). Moreover, the 95% confidence intervals, which range from 0.15 (a trivial effect) to 0.59 (a medium effect size), allow for a wide range of effect sizes individual trainees can expect.. Moreover, moderate levels of heterogeneity were detected, 69

which suggests that there may have been important differences between trials. One example of a potentially relevant difference is the training status of the participants. Since training can increase[11] one’s general muscle buffer capacity, it could be the case that the size of the effect of sodium bicarbonate supplementation varied according to the participants’ training status. However, this is speculative and a potential topic of investigation for future research. Supplementation side effects may also contribute towards greater heterogeneity. Some people experience gastrointestinal effects[12] from sodium bicarbonate supplementation, although the seriousness of these can vary greatly between individuals. Since gastric discomfort can negatively affect[13] exercise performance, it’s possible that greater ergogenic effects were found in trials where few or no participants experienced significant gastric discomfort, which may contribute toward increased heterogeneity. The absence of a significant effect of sodium bicarbonate on muscular strength also makes sense from a physiological standpoint. Muscular strength tests typically involve maximal exertion of effort that only lasts a fe

ignificant effect of sodium bicarbonate on muscular strength also makes sense from a physiological standpoint. Muscular strength tests typically involve maximal exertion of effort that only lasts a few seconds, while research[1] suggests that the ergogenic effects of bicarbonate are seen during exhaustive exercise lasting between 30 seconds and 10 minutes, during which period anaerobic glycolysis[14] plays a major role in energy provision. While it was hypothesized that ergogenic effects from sodium bicarbonate supplementation may have been more likely in a fatigued state due to possible H+ accumulation from previous exercise, the results of the meta- analysis did not detect such effects. Some of the strengths of the study under review include the use of a random-effects model to analyze the data (which is more accurate in detecting significant results than a fixed-effect model), the inclusion of only crossover trials (which has each participant act as their own control and, therefore, minimizes inter-individual variance), and the use of mostly trials of good or excellent methodological quality. However, the meta-analysis under review also has some limitations. First, it only inclu

minimizes inter-individual variance), and the use of mostly trials of good or excellent methodological quality. However, the meta-analysis under review also has some limitations. First, it only included a relatively small number of trials, most of which had small samples sizes. Second, there were moderate levels of heterogeneity between the included trials, which suggests that it may not have been appropriate to meta-analyze the data from these trials to begin with. Finally, out of the 192 total participants, 184 were men, which means that the results cannot necessarily be extrapolated to women. While the results of the study under review make sense from a physiological standpoint, the inclusion of a limited number of small trials with moderate levels of heterogeneity is an issue. 70

What’s the take-home? This meta-analysis suggests that, in healthy men, supplementation with 0.3-0.4 grams of sodium bicarbonate per kilogram of bodyweight 60 to 180 minutes before exercise will likely have a small positive effect on muscular endurance in both large and small muscle groups, but not on muscular strength, either in a rested or fatigued state. Larger trials are needed to confirm these findings, and more research is needed to investigate potential variations in efficacy in different populations. Supercharging muscle protein synthesis through essential amino acids Tags: EAAs, Muscle Protein Synthesis, Whey, Muscle Mass Study under review: Anabolic response to essential amino acid plus whey protein composition is greater than whey protein alone in young healthy adults. Quick Takes • What question this study addressed: How does a blend of whey protein and essential amino acids (EAAs) at two doses affect muscle anabolism compared to whey alone? • Who this study applies to: Young, healthy men and women. • What the intervention was: One or two doses of a commercial blend containing 3.2 grams of EAAs and 2.4 grams of protein per dose, compared to a commercial drink containing 12.6 grams of whey. • What the main takeaway is: All groups experienced a boost in protein synthesis, with the high-dose EAA/protein blend seeing the highest. • Caveats: Along with some open questions concerning study design and reporting, doses of EAAs, leucine, and whey protein in this study were all suboptimal. This is possibly due to the fact that this study tested commercial blends with set 73
mal. This is possibly due to the fact that this study tested commercial blends with set 73

ratios of these nutrients. Also, how the results found here relate to practical factors like lean mass and strength gains remains to be explored. What was the question? What is the protein synthesis response to a mixture of essential amino acids plus whey and a commercial drink (Gatorade Recover) containing 12.6 grams of whey? Why was the question worth asking? Changes in muscle mass are driven by changes in muscle protein synthesis[1] (MPS). New protein is constantly being created while older proteins are degraded. This is called protein turnover. To build muscle, the rate of muscle protein synthesis must exceed the rate of muscle protein breakdown. Muscle protein synthesis can be increased by eating protein or moving. However, there are several factors that determine how much MPS responds, including age, exercise, and nutrition. There are lots of reasons to want to maximize MPS, from offsetting muscle loss due to diseases such as cancer or diabetes,[2] to enhancing the hypertrophic response to training. Therefore, it could be useful for a wide range of people if researchers could identify a specific ingredient, dose, or mixture for maximizing MPS. Protein synthesis can be measure

esponse to training. Therefore, it could be useful for a wide range of people if researchers could identify a specific ingredient, dose, or mixture for maximizing MPS. Protein synthesis can be measured using multiple methods[3]. One is through measuring whole- body protein synthesis. Another involves measuring directly in the muscle, which is known as muscle protein fractional synthesis. These two measurements mostly agree in direction with each other, but the magnitude of change can be different. This is likely because muscle accounts for about 30% of whole-body protein synthesis and turns over at a much slower rate than other organs [4], like the gut or liver. The study under review used both methods, which helps determine the effects of the different protein mixtures in relation to older research that only used whole-body protein synthesis. Previous research has found that the post-meal rise in plasma essential amino acids[5] (EAA) or leucine[6] is important for the increase in MPS. It is currently recommended that 20-25 grams[7] (8-10 grams of EAA) of a rapidly-absorbed protein, like whey[8], can maximally stimulate MPS after resistance exercise in young healthy individuals. Ho

It is currently recommended that 20-25 grams[7] (8-10 grams of EAA) of a rapidly-absorbed protein, like whey[8], can maximally stimulate MPS after resistance exercise in young healthy individuals. However, whether adding EAA to the mix can help 74

even further is still unclear. This study helps fill that gap by addressing the possible superiority of untested mixtures of EAA plus whey. The authors argue that the amino acid with the lowest concentration relative to demand could limit the body’s anabolic response, even if other amino acids dosages are high. This is why combining a mixture of all EAAs with an intact protein could be beneficial. It is also well known that the protein synthetic response to whey is longer in duration than free EAA[9], but the spike in MPS with free- form essential amino acids is generally larger. So, if the two are combined there might be a further benefit for increasing protein synthesis. There is reason to suspect that adding essential amino acids (EAAs) to whey protein could improve muscle protein synthesis. However, optimal dosing is still unclear. This study explored how different doses of EAAs added on top of whey protein affects muscle protein synthesis. How was the question answered? This study used a randomized, two-period, stable isotope infusion in fasted and post-meal conditions. One group was randomized to drink a serving of an EAA and whey supplement mixture consisting of 3.2 grams of

used a randomized, two-period, stable isotope infusion in fasted and post-meal conditions. One group was randomized to drink a serving of an EAA and whey supplement mixture consisting of 3.2 grams of EAA and 2.4 grams of whey, or two servings of the same supplement, which equates to 6.4 grams of EAA and 4.8 grams of whey, then crossed over to take the other dose, as shown in Figure 1. In a separate arm, different participants were given 17.6 grams of Gatorade Recover, which contained 12.6 grams of whey protein. 75

Figure 1: Study design The primary outcome was the total anabolic response, which is measured by subtracting muscle protein breakdown from muscle protein synthesis. The secondary outcomes were muscle protein fractional synthetic rate and plasma amino acid concentration. The study involved 16 college-aged female and male participants. The protocol was preregistered with its prespecified primary outcomes being whole-body protein balance and changes in muscle protein synthesis. In this study, 16 college-aged participants were provided either a commercial drink containing 12.6 grams of whey protein, or a mix of EAA and whey containing 3.2 grams of EAA and 2.4 grams of whey, or 6.4 grams of EAA and 4.8 grams of whey. Muscle protein synthesis and whole-body protein balance were the primary 76

outcomes. What was the answer? Every group increased whole-body protein balance. The group consuming 12.6 grams of the EAA and whey mixture experienced a significant increase, while the results of the 6.3 gram EAA/whey group were roughly similar to the Gatorade Recover group. The total net changes in protein balance and the relative contributions to increasing protein synthesis and decreasing protein breakdown are shown in Figure 2. Figure 2: Net-body whole protein changes For muscle protein synthesis, only the 12.6 gram EAA/whey group significantly increased from baseline, while the 6.3 gram EAA/whey group and the Gatorade Recover group did not. 77

All conditions increased whole-body protein balance, with the higher dose of the EAA and whey combo having the biggest effect. Only the high-dose combo had a statistically significant effect on muscle protein synthesis. How much should you trust the answer? This study adds to the body of literature on protein synthesis because it used two doses of essential amino acids with whey that have not been tested directly, and compared them to a commercial whey protein product, Gatorade Recover. The authors found that a balanced, 12.6 gram mixture of essential amino acids with whey resulted in an improvement in net protein balance and fractional synthesis rate greater than that of a mixture of 6.3 grams of EAA with whey or Gatorade Recover. Essential amino acids can increase MPS at rest[10] and after strength training[11], while nonessential amino acids do not. Furthermore, a dose-response study found the optimal essential amino acid dose to maximally stimulate muscle protein fractional synthesis rate is about 10 grams [12], which somewhat agrees with the data presented in this study because a 12.6 gram mixture of EAA/whey, which contained 6.4 grams of free EAA, was the only treatment that

is rate is about 10 grams [12], which somewhat agrees with the data presented in this study because a 12.6 gram mixture of EAA/whey, which contained 6.4 grams of free EAA, was the only treatment that had a statistically significant effect on MPS. However, the EAA mixture used here is well short of the optimal dose, so there could have been an even better MPS response with a higher EAA content. This raises the question of why the authors did not use a larger dose. One possible reason could be because they are comparing commercial products, including the EAA product of their study’s sponsor, Adesso LLC. The submaximal EAA dose isn’t the only reason why there may be room for improvement in the tested proprietary blend. It is thought that leucine serves as a trigger for the MPS response. However, the high dose EAA/whey mixture only contained 128 milligrams of leucine, which is well below the proposed threshold of three grams of leucine[13] for a maximal MPS response. Using a somewhat similar experimental design, another study found a dose of 15 grams of EAA with about 2.8 grams of leucine increased MPS by about 60%[14], which is actually less than the increase reported here. However, a

ar experimental design, another study found a dose of 15 grams of EAA with about 2.8 grams of leucine increased MPS by about 60%[14], which is actually less than the increase reported here. However, an exact comparison is difficult because study participants had a higher baseline MPS due to some differences in experimental design. The anabolic response to protein is frequently thought of as a muscle response, but whole body response is also important to compare to earlier research in the field. Furthermore, the whole body 78

approach has been used extensively in protein research because it is less invasive and can measure both rates of protein synthesis and breakdown. Here, the authors found that the higher dose EAA plus whey mixture had a higher net balance due to an increase in whole-body protein synthesis and a reduction in muscle protein breakdown. This data falls in line with the literature, as the comparison of whole body protein synthesis and muscle protein synthesis can be different[15]. It also indicates that consuming EAA isn’t solely elevating protein synthesis in the muscle because other tissues can use them too. There is some debate about whether MPS reflects long term muscle growth. One study from 2014 found that MPS is not correlated with strength training[16], which is known to increase muscle mass. However, another study found that MPS does correlate with muscle growth[17]. Even more recently, a different group of researchers found muscle growth to correlate with an increase in MPS after three weeks of strength training[18]. These studies reveal more about MPS post-exercise than with EAA intake. In fact, there have been no MPS studies that compare EAA or whey to long term muscle growth

hree weeks of strength training[18]. These studies reveal more about MPS post-exercise than with EAA intake. In fact, there have been no MPS studies that compare EAA or whey to long term muscle growth. This leaves a difficult to answer question: would these acute MPS elevations translate to gaining muscle mass? It’s hard to say without longer-term data looking specifically at muscle mass. It is worth mentioning that the muscle specific protein synthesis methods are limited to some extent by the assumption that the responses at a single site may not reflect all skeletal muscle[19]. The study also does not indicate whether these participants were trained or untrained, which could play a role in how muscle protein fractional synthesis rates change[20] over the course of the measuring period. Another issue is that while the methods used in the study match those in the preregistration, the researchers reported 24 participants in the trial registration. It’s unclear why this discrepancy exists, but one possibility is that the authors counted the participants in the crossover trial twice in the preregistration. In addition, the pre-planned participant number was 30 and the authors didn’t

ts, but one possibility is that the authors counted the participants in the crossover trial twice in the preregistration. In addition, the pre-planned participant number was 30 and the authors didn’t reach it, and no explanation was given. It’s possible that the study was stopped short because significant differences were found. A power analysis could rule out this possibility, but if the authors did one, it wasn’t reported. Furthermore, the comparison to Gatorade Recover is odd because one serving contains only 12.6 grams of whey. This is lower than other ready-to-drink shakes or a standard scoop of whey powdered protein and may make the EAA/whey mixture look better than if it were compared to the recommended 25-30 gram[21] dose. While this study found that the higher-dose EAA/whey blend improved muscle protein synthesis, the dose used may still be suboptimal, and was tested as part of a proprietary blend. Also, the control of pure whey used a lower-than-optimal dose as well, 79

raising questions about how well the tested blend would compare to a higher dose of whey. Finally, whether the findings would translate to long term muscle growth without strength training is an open question. What’s the take-home? The authors found that a 12.6 gram mixture of EAA and whey increases muscle protein fractional synthesis beyond that of a 6.3 gram EAA/whey mixture or 17.6 grams of Gatorade Recover, which contains 12.6 grams of whey per serving. There were also changes in whole-body protein balance in all the groups, but the high dose EAA/whey group had the highest. However, the amount of EAA in the high-dose blend group was well below the optimal amount of EAA previously found to maximize muscle protein fractional synthesis, while the dose of whey it was compared to was also suboptimal. These issues, along with some questions about study design and reporting, suggest that these results should be taken as preliminary. 04 Insulin Sensitivity & Glucose Uptake How to use this Guide The team at Examine.com has been publishing research on nutrition and supplementation since March 2011. In that time, we’ve learned a great deal about supplements, especially how they can work together to help you with health goals. This stack guide help you figure out which supplements can help you and which will hinder and/or be a waste of your money for your desired goals. The following four sections present information on supplements that are relevant to Cardiovascular & Heart Health: 1. Base Supplements 2. Proven Options 3. Unproven Options 4. Cautionary and Overhyped Options Base Supplements are recommended for the majority of people with this goal. They are either effective on their own or are required to boost the effects of another supplement. These are the first supplements to consider for your stack. Base Supplements are more researched and have less adverse drug interactions than options. Proven Options are supplements that will provide a lot of benefits, but only in the right context. They cannot be recommended for everyone, but if you read the entry and find that you meet the criteria, feel free to add the

ements that will provide a lot of benefits, but only in the right context. They cannot be recommended for everyone, but if you read the entry and find that you meet the criteria, feel free to add the supplement to your stack. Unproven Options are another group of potentially beneficial supplements, but they lack evidence for their effects. They cannot be recommended with the same confidence as proven options. They could work or be a waste of your money - there is not enough evidence to know for sure. Keep unproven options in mind, but approach them cautiously when incorporating them into your stack. Cautionary and Overhyped Options are supplements that are claimed to provide benefits but have been shown to be ineffective. If a supplement is deemed too risky to be used, it will also be found in this section. Do not add these compounds to your stack; they tend to be a waste of money or potentially harmful to your health. 05 Insulin Sensitivity & Glucose Uptake Base Supplements Zinc Why you should take it Zinc is an important dietary mineral for general health. People with insulin resistance can benefit from zinc supplementation, but only if they are deficient in zinc. Supplementation will reduce insulin and glycated hemoglobin (HbA1c) levels in the body while improving insulin sensitivity. HbA1c is a biomarker for diabetes risk, which means high levels of HbA1c are correlated with diabetes, though HbA1c itself does not cause diabetes. The most reliable way to determine if you have a zinc deficiency is by keeping track of what you eat on a weekly basis, then comparing the zinc contents in your food to the recommended daily allowance of zinc for your age and gender. People with no insulin resistance and no zinc deficiency do not need to supplement zinc. Zinc is a base supplement only for insulin resistant people with diets low in zinc. How to take it To supplement zinc, take 25 – 30 mg of elemental zinc. Elemental zinc is the amount of zinc in the supplement you’re taking, excluding any compounds included to improve zinc absorption. For example, to take 30 mg of elemental zinc, take 230 mg of zinc gl

Elemental zinc is the amount of zinc in the supplement you’re taking, excluding any compounds included to improve zinc absorption. For example, to take 30 mg of elemental zinc, take 230 mg of zinc gluconate. The label displays the elemental zinc content, not the total dosage. Zinc should be taken with meals. Taking zinc on an empty stomach may cause nausea. Do not pair zinc with minerals like calcium, magnesium, and iron in combined doses of 800 mg or more. Combining them at low doses is fine, but in high amounts the minerals will compete for absorption and limit the overall effectiveness of supplementation. If your diet is high in zinc, you do not need to supplement zinc. Foods like meat, fish, beans, nuts, seeds, and eggs are high in zinc.

06 Insulin Sensitivity & Glucose Uptake Note: This dose is commonly recommended for athletic people who have high zinc losses in sweat. If you are either sedentary, don't produce a large amount of sweat, or have a diet moderate to high in meat products this dose could be dangerous for long-term daily usage. If that is the case, then reduce the daily dose to the range of 10-20 mg once daily. Myo-inositol Why you should take it Myo-inositol is a compound with a similar structure to glucose. It can improve insulin signaling because it is an ingredient in the reaction that creates phosphatidylinositol (3,4,5)-trisphosphate (PIP3). Myo-inositol deficiencies are associated with reduced PIP3 levels. Reduced PIP3 levels prevent insulin from signaling effectively. Supplementation of myo-inositol can benefit people suffering from type II diabetes or polycystic ovarian syndrome (PCOS), since it can improve insulin sensitivity. How to take it To supplement myo-inositol, assuming a powder supplement, take 4 g. To supplement with a soft gel formulation of myo-inositol, take 1,200 mg. Myo-inositol should be taken with food, though more research is needed to confirm that this is the best way to ta

t, take 4 g. To supplement with a soft gel formulation of myo-inositol, take 1,200 mg. Myo-inositol should be taken with food, though more research is needed to confirm that this is the best way to take myo-inositol. Pregnant women should not supplement myo-inositol. Myo-inositol supplementation can induce uterine contractions. Creatine Why you should take it Creatine is a source of fuel for muscle cells. Supplementing creatine will improve exercise performance and may benefit heart health. It also improves glucose uptake during muscle contractions, specifically during exercise. Base Supplements (cont.)

07 Insulin Sensitivity & Glucose Uptake Improved glucose uptake gives the muscles more energy to work with and staves off fatigue. Creatine is a base supplement for glucose uptake and insulin sensitivity because it’s cheap, safe, and does not interact with pharmaceuticals. People with no insulin resistance will still benefit from creatine supplementation. How to take it The best way to supplement creatine is to take creatine monohydrate. Other forms of creatine may be more expensive, but studies have not found them to be more effective than creatine monohydrate. If you are particularly sensitive to creatine’s digestive side-effects, which include nausea and cramping, consider supplementing micronized creatine, which may be gentler on the digestive system. The daily dose for creatine is 5 g a day. Loading creatine means taking a high dose of creatine for a short period of time before moving down to a smaller maintenance dose, which can be taken indefinitely. This is not necessary for effective supplementation. Though loading may result in benefits appearing slightly faster, results normalize after a few weeks. Some people are creatine nonresponders, which means creatine is unable to

fective supplementation. Though loading may result in benefits appearing slightly faster, results normalize after a few weeks. Some people are creatine nonresponders, which means creatine is unable to pass from their blood to their muscles. More research is needed to find a proven way to circumvent creatine nonresponse. Some evidence suggests it helps to take creatine with a meal high in both protein and carbohydrates, close to the time of actual muscle contraction. If you experience creatine nonresponse, consider taking creatine with a meal either before or after a workout. If you respond to creatine, you don’t have to worry about timing supplementation, though you will probably want to take it with a meal to lower the risk of an upset stomach. Base Supplements (cont.)

08 Insulin Sensitivity & Glucose Uptake Berberine Why it is a proven option Berberine is a compound derived from plants like Berberis aristata, Argemone mexicana, and Berberis aquifolium. These plants are claimed to be anti- diabetic due to their berberine content. Berberine lowers blood glucose levels by activating a protein called 5’ AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK). AMPK is responsible for drawing glucose into cells and signaling the body to convert the fuel to energy. This lowers blood sugar levels without interacting with insulin. Berberine supplementation will not cause low blood sugar levels, also known as hypoglycemia, like excess insulin does. Berberine cannot be recommended as a base supplement due to its interactions with various enzymes, including CYP3A4, CYP2C9, and CYP2D6. These enzymes metabolize many pharmaceuticals. It is possible that berberine could interact with various oral contraceptives. How to take it To supplement berberine, take 300 – 500 mg of berberine in three or four divided doses throughout the day, for a total daily dosage of 900 – 2,000 mg. Start at the low end of the range. People with insulin resistance should then slowly increase their berberi

three or four divided doses throughout the day, for a total daily dosage of 900 – 2,000 mg. Start at the low end of the range. People with insulin resistance should then slowly increase their berberine dose to 1,500 mg a day, in three divided doses of 500 mg a day. Berberine should be taken with or shortly after a carbohydrate-containing meal. High doses of berberine can cause stomach distress and diarrhea. Cinnamon Why it is a proven option Cinnamon, from the plant Cinnamomum cassia, can reduce blood sugar levels in healthy people when taken with carbohydrates. Cinnamon does not have Proven Options

09 Insulin Sensitivity & Glucose Uptake Proven Options (cont.) this effect in insulin-resistant people, though it does help maintain blood glucose levels between meals, if taken daily. Though multiple studies suggest cinnamon can reduce glucose and insulin levels, much more research is needed to determine the optimal dosing and timing to achieve this effect. Cinnamon can be included in this stack to improve the rate at which blood glucose is drawn into the muscles, as long as the user is not insulin resistant. How to take it To supplement cinnamon, take 5 – 6 g of Cinnamomum verum, also known as Ceylon cinnamon, or Cinnamomum cassia, also known as cassia cinnamon. Cinnamon should be taken throughout the day, with meals containing carbohydrates. Cinnamon can also be supplemented by brewing a tea or by purchasing cinnamon supplements. Brewing tea makes it difficult to supplement precise dosages. Cinnamon tea contains less coumarin than cinnamon supplements. Coumarin is a liver toxin that can be harmful in high doses. Tea reduces the risk of coumarin poisoning because the toxin is left behind in the dregs. Cinnamon supplements should be dosed based on their cinnamon content. For examp

can be harmful in high doses. Tea reduces the risk of coumarin poisoning because the toxin is left behind in the dregs. Cinnamon supplements should be dosed based on their cinnamon content. For example, to supplement 6 g of cinnamon, assuming a 10:1 cinnamon water extract supplement, take 600 mg of the cinnamon supplement.

10 Insulin Sensitivity & Glucose Uptake Isoleucine Why it is an unproven option Isoleucine is one of the three branched chain amino acids (BCAAs). Isoleucine supplementation can improve the rate at which blood sugar is taken up by muscle cells. This effect does not involve insulin or 5’ AMP- activated protein kinase (AMPK) signaling. Though there are a lot of studies on BCAAs and glucose levels, this evidence cannot be used to evaluate isoleucine by itself. Further research is needed to determine if high doses of isoleucine can cause low blood sugar, also known as hypoglycemia. Additional studies are also needed to determine what effect insulin resistance has on isoleucine supplementation. If you are healthy and not insulin resistant, many of the supplements in this guide will not be very effective for you. Isoleucine, however, can provide benefits for active and healthy people, especially those who have a high carbohydrate diet. How to take it To supplement isoleucine, take 72 mg per kilogram of bodyweight. This is approximately: • 4.9 g for a 150lb person • 6.5 g for a 200lb person • 8.1 g for a 250lb person This dose is based off of preliminary animal evidence. People eating at

kilogram of bodyweight. This is approximately: • 4.9 g for a 150lb person • 6.5 g for a 200lb person • 8.1 g for a 250lb person This dose is based off of preliminary animal evidence. People eating at least 60 g of protein a day or people supplementing BCAAs will need less leucine than described above. As a rule of thumb, aim for 5 g of leucine a day. Unproven Options

11 Insulin Sensitivity & Glucose Uptake Unproven Options (cont.) Corosolic Acid Why it is an unproven option Corosolic acid is a compound found in anti-diabetic plants like banaba leaf. Corosolic acid shares a mechanism (not the major one) with Berberine, another anti-diabetic compound found in plants. Corosolic acid can improve insulin signaling after supplementation. A lot more research is needed to confirm that corosolic acid supplementation is effective at improving insulin signaling. Preliminary evidence suggests it may be as potent as berberine at suppressing a protein called tyrosine-protein phosphatase non-receptor type 1 (PTP1B). Suppressing PTP1B gives insulin more time to act in the cells and improves its effectiveness. Though more human evidence is needed before corosolic acid can be recommended specifically for insulin sensitivity, corosolic acid supplementation could be useful for people concerned with insulin sensitivity who also want to encourage muscle growth. Anyone concerned with muscle growth can consider corosolic acid as an alternative to berberine, since berberine’s interaction with the 5’ AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK) receptor may interfere with muscle

oncerned with muscle growth can consider corosolic acid as an alternative to berberine, since berberine’s interaction with the 5’ AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK) receptor may interfere with muscle growth. How to take it To supplement corosolic acid, take 10 mg with a carbohydrate containing meal. Dosing corosolic acid supplements will depend on their corosolic acid content. For example, to supplement corosolic acid through an herbal supplement like banaba leaf, assuming a supplemental corosolic acid content of 3%, take 330 mg of the banaba leaf supplement.

12 Insulin Sensitivity & Glucose Uptake Unproven Options (cont.) Salacia Why it is an unproven option Salacia refers to the herbs, Salacia reticulate and Salacia oblonga. Salacia supplementation can prevent the absorption of up to 25% of ingested carbohydrates. It is more reliable than other carbohydrate absorption inhibitors because it acts on a variety of carbohydrate-digesting enzymes. Preventing the absorption of carbohydrates will reduce the increase in blood glucose and insulin that occurs after a meal. Over time, this kind of effect is beneficial for insulin resistant people. Preliminary evidence suggests salacia is as potent as acarbose, a pharmaceutical that blocks carbohydrate absorption. However, salacia cannot be specifically recommended over acarbose without additional evidence for its effects. How to take it To supplement salacia, take 240 – 1,000 mg of Salacia reticulata or Salacia oblonga. Salacia must be taken immediately before or after a meal containing carbohydrates, since its main mechanism involves reducing carbohydrate absorption. If supplemented by someone on a low carbohydrate diet, salacia will have no effect.
, since its main mechanism involves reducing carbohydrate absorption. If supplemented by someone on a low carbohydrate diet, salacia will have no effect.

13 Insulin Sensitivity & Glucose Uptake Cautionary & Overhyped Options Chromium Chromium supplementation is frequently claimed to increase insulin signaling. Chromium only has this effect when supplemented by people with a chromium deficiency. Outside chronic conditions and hospital emergencies, there has never been a reported case of insulin resistance caused by a chromium deficiency. Though chromium may have a mild effect on blood glucose levels when supplemented by people with type II diabetes, this effect is very small and does not provide benefits for other aspects of insulin resistance, like levels of fasted insulin. Since true chromium deficiencies are very rare, chromium should not be part of any supplement stack.

16 Insulin Sensitivity & Glucose Uptake Stack Modification FAQ How do I add supplements to my stack that are not covered in this guide? Before adding a new supplement to your stack, supplement your current stack for a few weeks to determine if you need to make a new addition. If you want to make multiple changes to your stack, pick one supplement to add at a time. Identify the stack change that you think will be the most effective, and do your research: 1. Use Examine.com to determine if that supplement would have a negative interaction with your current stack. Talk to your doctor about including a new supplement in your stack. 2. Introduce the new supplement at half of the regular dose. 3. After a week with the new supplement, slowly increase the dose to the recommended dose if you are not experiencing the effects you want. Stacks are intended to be synergistic, which means taking two supplements together may provide more effects than the supplements by themselves. New supplements should be added carefully, since even low doses can be powerful if other supplements in your stack improve their effects. Can I modify the recommended doses? If a supplement has an established advised do

uld be added carefully, since even low doses can be powerful if other supplements in your stack improve their effects. Can I modify the recommended doses? If a supplement has an established advised dosage range, stay within that range. If a supplement has a recommended dose, and not a range, stay within 10% of that dose. Halving or doubling an advised dose could be ineffective or even dangerous. The safest way to add dietary supplements to your life is one at a time. If you are considering purchasing several supplements, purchase only one and add the others after a week or two of supplementation. This will limit the risk of new supplements, and it will also make it easier to figure out what supplements are providing you with your newfound benefits.

17 Insulin Sensitivity & Glucose Uptake Stack Modification FAQ (cont.) Can any of these supplements cause low blood sugar? None of the above supplements, used safely, can cause low blood sugar, also known as hypoglycemia. It is possible for high doses of multiple supplements to have a synergistic effect resulting in hypoglycemia though. Cinnamon is most likely to cause hypoglycemia when taken alongside other supplements because it causes insulin secretion. When adding cinnamon to stacks, make sure it is consumed alongside some dietary carbohydrates.

18 Insulin Sensitivity & Glucose Uptake Precautions & Troubleshooting The safest way to add dietary supplements to your life is one at a time. If you are considering purchasing several supplements, purchase only one and add the others after a week or two of supplementation. This will limit the risk of new supplements, and it will also make it easier to figure out what supplements are providing you with your newfound benefits.
